Mew, or a Pokémon transformed into Mew, can use Genesis Supernova if it knows Psychic, holds Mewnium Z, and if its Trainer wears a Z-Ring or Z-Power Ring. Mew cannot use Genesis Supernova if it is transformed into a Pokémon other than Mew. Genesis Supernova is the only Z-Move that sets terrain of any kind.

Title: Shell Trap (move)

Shell Trap (Japanese: トラップシェル Trap Shell) is a damage-dealing Fire-type move introduced in Generation VII. It is the signature move of Turtonator. Shell Trap is a decreased priority move. The user sets up a trap at the beginning of the turn, then if it gets hit by an opponent's physical move within the same turn, it will use Shell Trap immediately after that move. Otherwise, when the user's scheduled turn happens at -3 priority, Shell Trap will fail at that time. Encore will fail if the target has an active Shell Trap in waiting. Shell Trap will not be triggered from the Pokémon being hit by an ally's move, having its substitute take a hit, being hit by a move that's receiving a bonus from Sheer Force, being hit after its own turn has already passed (such as by Dragon Tail or another move with priority lower than -3), or being hit by a move that causes it to faint. Due to a glitch, if a Pokémon's use of Shell Trap is preempted by Encore to lock it into another move instead, and the Pokémon is then hit by a physical attack on any turn before Encore ends, the Pokémon's next use of Shell Trap will skip the step of setting a trap, and the move will go off successfully at its normal priority regardless of whether it was hit in the current turn. If the user is affected by Powder and is then hit by an opponent's physical move, it will take damage instead.

Title: Fleur Cannon (move)

Fleur Cannon (Japanese: フルールカノン Fleur Cannon) is a damage-dealing Fairy-type move introduced in Generation VII. It is the signature move of Magearna. Fleur Cannon deals damage and lowers the user's Special Attack by two stages. In Versions 1.0 to 1.1.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Fleur Cannon could not be selected in a battle. Starting with Version 1.2.0, it can be selected and functions as it did in the previous generation. Due to a bug, Original Color Magearna's unique animation for Fleur Cannon was not used in Versions 1.2.0 to 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield. This was fixed in Version 1.3.0. Sygna Suit Gladion & Magearna's passive skill Just Give It Up Already prevents this move from lowering their Sp. Atk after use.

Title: Psychic Fangs (move)

Psychic Fangs (Japanese: サイコファング Psycho Fang) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced in Generation VII. Prior to Generation VIII, it was the signature move of Bruxish, but other Pokémon could still inherit it via breeding. Psychic Fangs removes Light Screen, Reflect, and Aurora Veil from the target's side of the field, then inflicts damage. It will not remove Light Screen, Reflect, or Aurora Veil if Psychic Fangs misses or the target is immune to or protected from Psychic Fangs. In Pokémon GO, Psychic Fangs is a Charged Attack that was made available on November 21, 2021.

Title: Stomping Tantrum (move)

Stomping Tantrum (Japanese: じだんだ Stomping Tantrum) is a damage-dealing Ground-type move introduced in Generation VII. It was TM98 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and is TM084 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Stomping Tantrum is a damage-dealing move. If, on the previous turn, the user's last move missed, failed to affect each of its targets, or was prevented from being used due to an effect such as paralysis, Stomping Tantrum's power is doubled from 75 to 150. If the user was recharging, grabbed by Sky Drop, or any target of the user's last move blocked it with a protection move such as Protect on the previous turn, Stomping Tantrum's base power is not doubled. Stomping Tantrum deals damage. Its damage is increased if the move the user last used was unsuccessful. Description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021): Its power is doubled if the user's previous move failed. Description prior to Version 2.20.0 (from April 27, 2022): This move's power is doubled when the user's previous move failed.

Title: Shadow Bone (move)

Shadow Bone (Japanese: シャドーボーン Shadow Bone) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type move introduced in Generation VII. It is one of the signature moves of Alolan Marowak. Shadow Bone does damage and has a 20% chance of lowering the target's Defense stat by one stage. In Versions 1.0 to 1.1.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Shadow Bone could not be selected in a battle, as no Pokémon available in the game could learn it. Starting with Version 1.2.0, it is usable and functions as it did in the previous generation. Description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021): Has a small chance of lowering the target's Defense.

Title: Accelerock (move)

Accelerock (Japanese: アクセルロック Accelerock) is a damage-dealing Rock-type move introduced in Generation VII. It is the signature move of Midday Form and Dusk Form Lycanroc. Accelerock inflicts damage. It has a priority of +1, so it is used before all moves that do not have increased priority.

Title: Liquidation (move)

Liquidation (Japanese: アクアブレイク Aqua Break) is a damage-dealing Water-type move introduced in Generation VII. Liquidation does damage and has a 20% chance of lowering the target's Defense stat by one stage. Liquidation deals damage, and may lower the target's Defense by one stage. Description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021): Has a small chance of lowering the target's Defense. In Pokémon GO, Liquidation is a Charged Attack that has been available since June 1, 2023.

Title: Prismatic Laser (move)

Prismatic Laser (Japanese: プリズムレーザー Prism Laser) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced in Generation VII. It is one of the signature moves of Necrozma. Prismatic Laser inflicts damage and then forces the user to recharge during the next turn. The user does not have to recharge if this attack misses, or if the target is immune to or protected from the attack. In Versions 1.0 to 2.0.2 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Prismatic Laser could not be selected in a battle. Starting with Version 3.0.0, it is usable and functions the same as it did in previous generations. Prismatic Laser has the highest base power of all Psychic-type moves, excluding Z-Moves. A Shattered Psyche based on Prismatic Laser or Psycho Boost has the highest base power of all Psychic-type moves. It is also the only move that requires recharging to have 10 PP.

Title: Spectral Thief (move)

Spectral Thief (Japanese: シャドースチール Shadow Steal) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type move introduced in Generation VII. It is the signature move of Marshadow. It can be upgraded to the special Z-Move Soul-Stealing 7-Star Strike by Marshadow holding Marshadium Z. Spectral Thief deals damage. If successful, it also steals the target's positive stat stages (if any) immediately before dealing damage. Spectral Thief bypasses substitutes, and can successfully steal stat boosts from the boss of a Max Raid Battle even if it currently has a shield up. If the target has an Ability such as Clear Body, White Smoke, or Hyper Cutter that normally prevents some or all of its stats from being lowered, Spectral Thief can still steal boosts in those stats. Spectral Thief will steal all positive stat stages; even if the sum of the user's existing stat stages and the target's stat stages exceeds +6 in a stat, as long as the target's stat stages are positive, they will be reduced to 0. If the user has the Ability Simple, then the stolen stat stages will be doubled; if the user has the Ability Contrary, then the stat boosts will become stat drops when stolen. In I Choose You!, Marshadow was confirmed to know Spectral Thief through its use of Soul-Stealing 7-Star Strike. However, it has never been seen using it. If Battle Effects are turned off, Spectral Thief will still display one if it steals any stat boosts.

Title: Sunsteel Strike (move)

Sunsteel Strike (Japanese: メテオドライブ Meteor Drive) is a damage-dealing Steel-type move introduced in Generation VII. It is the signature move of Solgaleo and Dusk Mane Necrozma. In Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, it can be upgraded to the special Z-Move Searing Sunraze Smash by Solgaleo or Dusk Mane Necrozma holding Solganium Z. Sunsteel Strike inflicts damage, ignoring all ignorable Abilities during the execution of that move. (Ignorable Abilities are most Abilities that could potentially negatively affect the success, damage, or effects of a move if possessed by the target of a move or its allies—such as Multiscale and Friend Guard.) However, it does not ignore Abilities if it is called by another move rather than directly used. In Versions 1.0 to 2.0.2 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Sunsteel Strike could not be selected in a battle. Starting with Version 3.0.0, it is usable and functions the same as it did in previous generations. In Pokémon GO, Sunsteel Strike is a Charged Attack that has been available since May 30, 2024. Its Adventure Effect outside of battle, by using Necrozma Candy and Stardust, temporarily allows any Pokémon that evolve during daylight to evolve, as well as Dusk Form Rockruff. It can't be used simultaneously with an Incense, Mystery Box, or Coin Bag. It can also summon certain Pokémon. It costs three Necrozma Candy and 3,000 Stardust for 10 minutes. Description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021): Ignores passive skills that would reduce the damage of this attack. Ignores passive skills that would protect the target against a critical hit. Opponents cannot endure this attack.

Title: Moongeist Beam (move)

Moongeist Beam (Japanese: シャドーレイ Shadow Ray) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type move introduced in Generation VII. It is the signature move of Lunala and Dawn Wings Necrozma. In Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, it can be upgraded to the exclusive Z-Move Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom by Lunala or Dawn Wings Necrozma holding Lunalium Z. Moongeist Beam inflicts damage, ignoring all ignorable Abilities during the execution of that move. (Ignorable Abilities are most Abilities that could potentially negatively affect the success, damage, or effects of a move if possessed by the target of a move or its allies—such as Multiscale and Friend Guard.) However, it does not ignore Abilities if it is called by another move rather than directly used. In Versions 1.0 to 2.0.2 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Moongeist Beam could not be selected in a battle. Starting with Version 3.0.0, it is usable and functions the same as it did in previous generations. In Pokémon GO, Moongeist Beam is a Charged Attack that has been available since May 30, 2024. Its Adventure Effect outside of battle, by using Necrozma candy and Stardust, temporarily allows any Pokémon that evolve at night to evolve, including Dusk Rockruff and Ursaring without a full moon. It can also summon certain Pokémon. It can't be used simultaneously as an Incense, Mystery Box or Coin Bag. It costs 3 Necrozma candy and 3,000 Stardust for 10 minutes.

Title: Tearful Look (move)

Tearful Look (Japanese: なみだめ Teary Eyes) is a non-damaging Normal-type move introduced in Generation VII. Tearful Look lowers the target's Attack and Special Attack stats by one stage each. It bypasses accuracy checks to always hit, unless the opponent is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly. Tearful Look will hit the target even if it has used Protect, Detect, Spiky Shield, or Baneful Bunker, but will be blocked by Crafty Shield. If powered up by a Normalium Z into Z-Tearful Look, the user's Defense stat rises by one stage. Tearful Look lowers the target's Attack and Sp. Atk by one stage each. The stock image used in the battle animation of Tearful Look appears to be based on Espurr's eyes, which can't itself learn the move. This trait is shared with Baby-Doll EyesVII+ and Mean LookVII+.

Title: Zing Zap (move)

Zing Zap (Japanese: びりびりちくちく Shocking and Prickly) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced in Generation VII. Prior to Generation VIII, it was the signature move of Togedemaru. In Generation IX, it is the signature move of Pincurchin. Zing Zap inflicts damage and has a 30% chance of making the target flinch. Description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021): Has a moderate chance of making the target flinch.

Title: Mind Blown (move)

Mind Blown (Japanese: ビックリヘッド Surprised Head) is a damage-dealing Fire-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. It is the signature move of Blacephalon. Mind Blown inflicts damage, and the user takes damage equal to half of its maximum HP rounded up (unless it has Magic Guard). Mind Blown cannot be used when a Pokémon with the Ability Damp is on the field. The user of Mind Blown will take damage even if the target is protected from it, if it hits a substitute, if the move misses, or if the target has Flash Fire. If Mind Blown fails due to there being no targets (i.e. if all Pokémon in range fainted before the move was used), the user does not take damage. Rock Head and Reckless do not affect Mind Blown. If the user has Parental Bond in a Single Battle, the HP loss occurs after the first hit, and the second hit will only occur if neither Pokémon has fainted as a result. There is no additional HP loss for the second hit in that case. The user loses if all of the last Pokémon faint in the same turn as the result of using Mind Blown. In Versions 1.0 to 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Mind Blown could not be selected in a battle. Starting with Version 1.3.0, it is usable and functions the same as it did in the previous generation. Mind Blown does not exist in the game data of Pokémon Sun and Moon. As a result, a Pokémon knowing Mind Blown cannot be traded from Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon to Sun and Moon, and if a Pokémon in Pokémon Bank knows the move it cannot be withdrawn in Pokémon Sun and Moon. In order to be transferred to Pokémon Sun and Moon, the Pokémon must forget Mind Blown. The following table lists the probability that Strange Flames Mind Blown will increase the user's Special Moves ↑ Next effect by each number of ranks. In Pokémon GO, Mind Blown is a Charged Attack that has been available since December 31, 2025. If a Shiny Pokémon uses Mind Blown, the move's animation is different, appearing to use a Shiny Blacephalon's blue head rather than the normal white one. It shares this distinction with Dragon Darts and Syrup Bomb. This does not happen in Pokémon GO; the head will always be white regardless if the user is shiny.

Title: Plasma Fists (move)

Plasma Fists (Japanese: プラズマフィスト Plasma Fist) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. It is the signature move of Zeraora. Plasma Fists inflicts damage. In the remainder of the current turn, all Normal-type moves will become Electric-type, including status moves. This includes Max Moves, which will turn into Max Lightning (or G-Max Volt Crash or G-Max Stun Shock for Gigantamax Pikachu and Gigantamax Toxtricity, respectively). The type-changing effect is applied after move type-changing Abilities, so it cannot affect an originally Normal-type move used by a Pokémon with Pixilate but can affect an originally Fairy-type move used by a Pokémon with Normalize. Plasma Fists does not exist in the game data of Pokémon Sun and Moon. As a result, a Pokémon knowing Plasma Fists cannot be traded from Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon to Sun and Moon, and if a Pokémon in Pokémon Bank knows the move, it cannot be withdrawn in Pokémon Sun and Moon. In order to be transferred to Pokémon Sun and Moon, the Pokémon must forget Plasma Fists. In Pokémon UNITE, Zeraora's Unite Move, Plasma Gale, is similar to Plasma Fists.

Title: Photon Geyser (move)

Photon Geyser (Japanese: フォトンゲイザー Photon Geyser) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. It is one of the signature moves of Necrozma. It can be upgraded to the special Z-Move Light That Burns the Sky by Ultra Necrozma holding Ultranecrozium Z. Photon Geyser deals damage. If the user's Attack stat is higher than its Special Attack stat, Photon Geyser becomes a physical move, doing damage to the target's Defense stat; otherwise it is a special move, doing damage to the target's Special Defense stat. During the execution of Photon Geyser, all ignorable Abilities are ignored. (Ignorable Abilities are most Abilities that could potentially [negatively] affect the success, damage, or effects of a move if possessed by the target of a move or its allies, such as Multiscale and Friend Guard.) However, it does not ignore Abilities if it is called by another move rather than directly used. For determining which stat is higher, stat stage modifiers are taken into account, but other effects (e.g. held items such as a Choice Band, Abilities such as Huge Power, etc.) are not. In Versions 1.0 to 2.0.2 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Photon Geyser could not be selected in a battle. Starting with Version 3.0.0, it is usable and functions the same as it did in previous generations. Photon Geyser does not exist in the game data of Pokémon Sun and Moon. As a result, a Pokémon knowing Photon Geyser cannot be traded from Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon to Sun and Moon, and if a Pokémon in Pokémon Bank knows the move, it cannot be withdrawn in Pokémon Sun and Moon. In order to be transferred to Pokémon Sun and Moon, the Pokémon must forget Photon Geyser.

Title: Searing Sunraze Smash (move)

Searing Sunraze Smash (Japanese: サンシャインスマッシャー Sunshine Smasher) is a damage-dealing Steel-type Z-Move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. It is the exclusive Z-Move of Solgaleo and Dusk Mane Necrozma and an upgraded version of their signature move, Sunsteel Strike. Searing Sunraze Smash inflicts damage, ignoring all ignorable Abilities during the execution of that move. (Ignorable Abilities are most Abilities that could potentially negatively affect the success, damage, or effects of a move if possessed by the target of a move or its allies—such as Multiscale and Friend Guard.) Searing Sunraze Smash cannot be selected in a battle. Solgaleo or Dusk Mane Necrozma, or a Pokémon transformed into either, can use Searing Sunraze Smash if it knows Sunsteel Strike, holds a Solganium Z, and if its Trainer wears a Z-Power Ring. Searing Sunraze Smash and a Corkscrew Crash based on Doom Desire have the highest base power of all Steel-type moves.

Title: Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom (move)

Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom (Japanese: ムーンライトブラスター Moonlight Blaster) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type Z-Move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. It is the exclusive Z-Move of Lunala and Dawn Wings Necrozma and an upgraded version of their signature move, Moongeist Beam. Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom inflicts damage, ignoring all ignorable Abilities during the execution of that move. (Ignorable Abilities are most Abilities that could potentially negatively affect the success, damage, or effects of a move if possessed by the target of a move or its allies—such as Multiscale and Friend Guard.) Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom cannot be selected in a battle. Lunala or Dawn Wings Necrozma, or a Pokémon transformed into either, can use Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom if it knows Moongeist Beam, holds a Lunalium Z, and if its Trainer wears a Z-Power Ring. Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom and Soul-Stealing 7-Star Strike are tied for the longest English names of all moves, with 27 characters each.

Title: Splintered Stormshards (move)

Splintered Stormshards (Japanese: ラジアルエッジストーム Radial Edgestorm) is a damage-dealing Rock-type Z-Move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. It is the exclusive Z-Move of Lycanroc and an upgraded version of its Stone Edge. Splintered Stormshards inflicts damage and removes any terrain present on the battlefield. Splintered Stormshards cannot be selected in a battle. Any form of Lycanroc, or a Pokémon transformed into one, can use Splintered Stormshards if it knows Stone Edge, holds a Lycanium Z, and if its Trainer wears a Z-Power Ring. This sync move could not be raised to 6★ EX until January 25, 2023, which is 1091 days after its release date (on January 30, 2020). Description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021): Removes physical and special damage-reducing effects from the opponents' field of play. While the move's properties remain the same, the animation for Splintered Stormshards changes based on the form of Lycanroc using it, with the apparent time of day during the animation matching the Lycanroc's form. In Pokémon Masters EX, Island Splintered Stormshards is the only sync move that removes some field effects.

Title: Clangorous Soulblaze (move)

Clangorous Soulblaze (Japanese: ブレイジングソウルビート Blazing Soulbeat) is a damage-dealing Dragon-type Z-Move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. It is the exclusive Z-Move of Kommo-o and an upgraded version of its signature move, Clanging Scales. Clangorous Soulblaze inflicts damage to all opposing Pokémon and increases the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats by one stage each. Clangorous Soulblaze can hit Pokémon even if they are behind a substitute. The user's move fails if the opponents are in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move, or if the targets are immune to this move; the user's stats are not raised if the move is unable to damage any of the opposing Pokémon. The stats raised are part of the Z-Move's effect, rather than a Z-Power Effect, and therefore interacts with move-modifying effects such as Simple and Contrary normally. Clangororus Soulblaze cannot be selected in a battle. Kommo-o, or a Pokémon transformed into Kommo-o, can use Clangorous Soulblaze if it knows Clanging Scales, holds a Kommonium Z, and if its Trainer wears a Z-Power Ring. Does damage and grants stat boosts at the same time. In the animated series, Clangorous Soulblaze is the only Z-Move that: Debuted outside of Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon. Though Z-Moves have been removed from the core series as of Generation VIII, Kommo-o received a new signature move in this generation, Clangorous Soul; due to having a similar name in all languages, it could be seen as a downgraded version of Clangorous Soulblaze, with the similar effect of increasing the user's stats, but with the drawback of decreasing the user's HP.

Title: Zippy Zap (move)

Zippy Zap (Japanese: ばちばちアクセル Crackling Accel) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves of the partner Pikachu. Zippy Zap inflicts damage and will always result in a critical hit. It has a priority of +2, so it is used before all moves that do not have increased priority. Zippy Zap cannot be selected in a battle. However, it has been modified: its PP was changed to 10 while its power was changed to 80. Additionally, its secondary effect is raising evasion by one stage, instead of a guaranteed critical hit. In Let's Go, Pikachu!, the Move Tutor of Zippy Zap is located in Cerulean City's Pokémon Center. Zippy Zap is currently the last move in alphabetical order.

Title: Splishy Splash (move)

Splishy Splash (Japanese: ざぶざぶサーフ Splashing Surf) is a damage-dealing Water-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves of the partner Pikachu. Splishy Splash deals damage and has a 30% chance of paralyzing the target. In Let's Go, Pikachu!, the Move Tutor of Splishy Splash is located in Fuchsia City's Pokémon Center. Splishy Splash is the only Water-type move that can inflict paralysis. Splishy Splash may be a reference to Surfing Pikachu.

Title: Floaty Fall (move)

Floaty Fall (Japanese: ふわふわフォール Floaty Fall) is a damage-dealing Flying-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves of the partner Pikachu. Floaty Fall deals damage and has a 30% chance of making the target flinch. In Let's Go, Pikachu!, the Move Tutor of Floaty Fall is located in Celadon City's Pokémon Center. This move appears to be a reference to Flying Pikachu.

Title: Pika Papow (move)

Pika Papow (Japanese: ピカピカサンダー Pikapika Thunder) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves and the partner power of the partner Pikachu. It acts as a fifth move that can only be used by shaking the game controller when prompted by an animated icon of Pikachu waving a Joy-Con. Pika Papow deals damage and bypasses accuracy checks to always hit. The base power of Pika Papow is dependent on the friendship of the user. The greater the user's friendship, the greater the base power. The base power of Pika Papow varies between 1 (at the minimum friendship value of 0) and 102 (at the maximum friendship value of 255).

Title: Bouncy Bubble (move)

Bouncy Bubble (Japanese: いきいきバブル Lively Bubble) is a damage-dealing Water-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves of the partner Eevee. Bouncy Bubble deals damage and 50% of the damage dealt to the target is restored to the user. Bouncy Bubble cannot be selected in a battle. However, it has been modified: its PP was changed to 20, its power was changed to 60, and 100% of the damage dealt to the target is restored to the user. The move would be unusable if the user is under the Heal Block status. In Let's Go, Eevee!, the Move Tutor of Bouncy Bubble is located in Cerulean City's Pokémon Center. Bouncy Bubble is the only Water-type HP-draining move. Of all damage-dealing moves, Bouncy Bubble is tied with Quick Attack for most variations in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, with eight including itself.

Title: Buzzy Buzz (move)

Buzzy Buzz (Japanese: びりびりエレキ Shocking Electricity) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves of the partner Eevee. Buzzy Buzz deals damage and paralyzes its target when it hits. Buzzy Buzz cannot be selected in a battle. However, it has been modified: its PP was changed to 20 while its power was changed to 60. In Let's Go, Eevee!, the Move Tutor of Buzzy Buzz is located in Cerulean City's Pokémon Center. Even though this move cannot be used in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, one of the obtainable camera effects in the League Club Room is named after Buzzy Buzz. It shares this trait with Sizzly Slide.

Title: Sizzly Slide (move)

Sizzly Slide (Japanese: めらめらバーン Blazing Burn) is a damage-dealing Fire-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves of the partner Eevee. Sizzly Slide deals damage and burns its target when it hits. Sizzly Slide cannot be selected in a battle. However, it has been modified: its PP was changed to 20 while its power was changed to 60. In Let's Go, Eevee!, the Move Tutor of Sizzly Slide is located in Cerulean City's Pokémon Center. Even though this move cannot be used in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, one of the obtainable camera effects in the League Club Room is named after Sizzly Slide. It shares this trait with Buzzy Buzz.

Title: Glitzy Glow (move)

Glitzy Glow (Japanese: どばどばオーラ Gushing Aura) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves of the partner Eevee. Glitzy Glow deals damage and sets up the effects of the move Light Screen on the user's side of the field. Glitzy Glow cannot be selected in a battle. However, it has been modified: its power was changed to 80 and its accuracy to 95%. In Let's Go, Eevee!, the Move Tutor of Glitzy Glow is located in Celadon City's Pokémon Center.

Title: Baddy Bad (move)

Baddy Bad (Japanese: わるわるゾーン Bad-Bad Zone) is a damage-dealing Dark-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves of the partner Eevee. Baddy Bad deals damage and sets up the effects of the move Reflect on the user's side of the field. Baddy Bad cannot be selected in battle. However, it has been modified: its power was changed to 80 and its accuracy to 95%. In Let's Go, Eevee!, the Move Tutor of Baddy Bad is located in Celadon City's Pokémon Center.

Title: Sappy Seed (move)

Sappy Seed (Japanese: すくすくボンバー Fast-Growing Bomber) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves of the partner Eevee. Sappy Seed deals damage and plants a seed on the target. Sappy Seed has 90 power, 100% accuracy, and 15 PP. Sappy Seed cannot be selected in a battle. However, it has been modified: it now has 100 power, 90% accuracy, and 10 PP. In Let's Go, Eevee!, the Move Tutor of Sappy Seed is located in Fuchsia City's Pokémon Center.

Title: Freezy Frost (move)

Freezy Frost (Japanese: こちこちフロスト Frozen-Stiff Frost) is a damage-dealing Ice-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves of the partner Eevee. Freezy Frost deals damage and resets the stat stages of all active Pokémon on the field to 0. Freezy Frost cannot be selected in a battle. However, it has been modified: its power was changed to 100, its accuracy to 90%, and its PP to 10. In Let's Go, Eevee!, the Move Tutor of Freezy Frost is located in Fuchsia City's Pokémon Center.

Title: Sparkly Swirl (move)

Sparkly Swirl (Japanese: きらきらストーム Sparkly Storm) is a damage-dealing Fairy-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves of the partner Eevee. Sparkly Swirl deals damage and cures the user, all Pokémon in the user's party, and the user's allies, of all non-volatile status conditions (sleep, poison, paralysis, freeze, and burn). Sparkly Swirl cannot be selected in a battle. However, it has been modified: its power was changed to 120, its accuracy to 85%, and its PP to 5. In Let's Go, Eevee!, the Move Tutor of Sparkly Swirl is located in Fuchsia City's Pokémon Center.

Title: Veevee Volley (move)

Veevee Volley (Japanese: ブイブイブレイク Vuivui Break) is a damage-dealing Normal-type move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is one of the signature moves and the partner power of the partner Eevee. It acts as a fifth move that can only be used by shaking the game controller when prompted by an animated icon of Eevee waving a Joy-Con. Veevee Volley deals damage and bypasses accuracy checks to always hit. The base power of Veevee Volley is dependent on the friendship of the user. The greater the user's friendship, the greater the base power. The base power of Veevee Volley varies between 1 (at the minimum friendship value of 0) and 102 (at the maximum friendship value of 255). The base power of Veevee Volley is set to 1 if the formula outputs 0.

Title: Double Iron Bash (move)

Double Iron Bash (Japanese: ダブルパンツァー Double Panzer) is a damage-dealing Steel-type multistrike move introduced during Generation VII, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It is the signature move of Melmetal. Double Iron Bash inflicts damage, hitting the target twice per use, and has a 30% chance of causing the target to flinch with each hit (for an overall 51% chance). If a Focus Sash activates before the last strike is dealt, the following strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can still activate repeatedly to prevent fainting, but each chance is independent. Sturdy will activate with each hit if the defending Pokémon has a maximum HP of 1. If the target has used Minimize, Double Iron Bash deals double its regular damage and bypasses accuracy checks to always hit, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly. Double Iron Bash no longer has a special interaction with Minimize. Double Iron Bash is the last Generation VII move in index number order. In Pokémon the Series, Professor Kukui named this move. The Japanese name of this move plays on a pun between ‘Panzer’ (パンツァー, pantsaa) and ‘Punch’ (パンチ, panchi). This is supported by the fact that the move is categorized as a punching move. Double Iron Bash is the only move introduced in Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! that is usable in Sword and Shield. It is also the only move introduced in Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! to have been used in the Pokémon animated series.

Title: Max Guard (move)

Max Guard (Japanese: ダイウォール Dai Wall) is a non-damaging Normal-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Guard protects the user from moves for the rest of the turn. It offers greater protection than most other protection moves. Max Guard fully protects the user from most Max Moves and cannot be penetrated or nullified by most moves that break protection. G-Max One Blow, G-Max Rapid Flow, and Feint deal full damage through Max Guard, but Feint does not lift Max Guard's protection like it would other protection effects. Max Guard also follows many of the moves that bypass other protection, but it successfully protects from Block, Flower Shield, Gear Up, Magnetic Flux, Phantom Force, Psych Up, Shadow Force, Teatime, Transform, and any contact move affected by Unseen Fist. If the user goes last in the turn, Max Guard will fail. The chance that Max Guard will succeed drops each time the user successfully and consecutively uses Endure, any protection move that only affects the user, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard. Any Dynamax or Gigantamax Pokémon can use Max Guard if it knows a status move. All Dynamax and Gigantamax Pokémon can learn Max Guard. Crowned Sword Zacian, Crowned Shield Zamazenta, and Eternatus can learn a variant called Guard. Players must first unlock Max Guard and can then increase its level up to Level 3. Activating Dynamax Cannon's Adventure Effect temporarily increases Max Guard's level by 1, except for Zacian and Zamazenta. If Max Guard is locked, Dynamax Cannon will temporarily unlock it at Level 1. Using Max Guard during a Max Battle adds a small amount of temporary HP to the user, visually represented by a shield next to the user's HP bar. At Level 1, each shield represents 20 HP; at Level 2, each shield represents 40 HP; at Level 3, each shield represents 60 HP; and at Level 4 (Level 3 while under the Adventure Effect of Dynamax Cannon), each shield represents 80 HP. Using Max Guard repeatedly will add more shields, up to a maximum of three, for a combined maximum of 60 HP at Level 1, 120 HP at Level 2, 180 HP at Level 3, and 240 HP at Level 4. Max Guard will also make the user the center of attention, encouraging the boss to attack the user when using single-target attacks instead of picking a target at random. If Guard is unlocked on Crowned Shield Zamazenta, it will enter the battle with one shield pre-applied, immediately making it the center of attention. Crowned Shield Zamazenta can also have up to four shields at once, instead of the usual three, for a combined maximum of 80 HP at Level 1, 160 HP at Level 2, and 240 HP at Level 3. Zamazenta's Level 3 Guard has the same maximum shield of 240 HP as a Max Pokémon's Level 4 Max Guard, which is the highest possible since Zamazenta is not affected by Dynamax Cannon's Adventure Effect. Max Guard is the only Max Move in the core series: That can have a different type than its base move. Max Guard is the first Generation VIII move in index number order.

Title: Dynamax Cannon (move)

Dynamax Cannon (Japanese: ダイマックスほう Daimax Cannon) is a damage-dealing Dragon-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is one of the signature moves of Eternatus. Dynamax Cannon deals damage. If the target is Dynamaxed or Gigantamaxed, its damage is doubled. The bonus does not apply to Eternamax Eternatus, despite its similarities to a Gigantamaxed Pokémon. Encore fails if the target's last move was Dynamax Cannon. As there is no Dynamax mechanic in Generation IX, Dynamax Cannon now deals damage with no secondary effect. In Pokémon GO, Dynamax Cannon is a Charged Attack that debuted on August 17, 2025. Dynamax Cannon can also be used in Max Battles as a Max Move, and can be leveled up as one. Dynamax Cannon can't be replaced with another move using Charged TMs. Its Adventure Effect outside of battle will increase the level of all Max Moves by 1 (excluding Behemoth Blade and Behemoth Bash). Any Max Moves at Level 3 will increase to Level 4, which provides an exceptionally large amount of power relative to a standard level up, while any locked Max Moves will temporarily be raised to Level 1. It costs 5,000 and 30, and lasts 10 minutes. Prior to Version 2.20.0*: This move's power increases 50% when the target has a sync buff.

Title: Snipe Shot (move)

Snipe Shot (Japanese: ねらいうち Snipe Shot) is a damage-dealing Water-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Inteleon. Snipe Shot deals damage and has an increased critical hit ratio. It is unaffected by moves and Ability effects that draw in moves, such as Follow Me and Storm Drain. Prior to Version 1.2.0 of Sword and Shield, if Snipe Shot was selected by Metronome, the move would fail (as Metronome targets oneself when it is used). In Double Battles, due to a glitch, Snipe Shot will fail if called via Sleep Talk. In Pokémon Masters EX, this has the lowest power of all attack moves that cost four move-gauge slots.

Title: Jaw Lock (move)

Jaw Lock (Japanese: くらいつく Seize with Teeth) is a damage-dealing Dark-type move introduced in Generation VIII. Prior to Generation IX, it was the signature move of Chewtle and Drednaw. Jaw Lock inflicts damage. It also prevents the user and the target from fleeing or switching out (even if they are allies), unless either of them is already prevented, and until either Pokémon is removed from the field. Jaw Lock does not prevent a Pokémon from switching out with Wimp Out or Emergency Exit or due to Shed Shell, Eject Button, or Red Card.

Title: Stuff Cheeks (move)

Stuff Cheeks (Japanese: ほおばる Stuff Cheeks) is a non-damaging Normal-type move introduced in Generation VIII. Prior to Generation IX, it was the signature move of Skwovet and Greedent. The user eats its held Berry, and raises its Defense stat by two stages in addition to the effect that Berry might normally have. The user can successfully eat its Berry even in the presence of Unnerve or Magic Room. If the user is not holding a Berry, Stuff Cheeks cannot be selected from the move menu, and if the user has no other usable moves, it will be forced to use Struggle. If a Pokémon would use Stuff Cheeks, but is not holding a Berry when it would use the move (e.g. if its held Berry had been removed by Knock Off), Stuff Cheeks fails. In Pokémon UNITE, Stuff Cheeks is Greedent's second move. It is obtained by evolving from Skwovet at level 5 and upgrading Defense Curl into it instead of Covet. The user drops several Oran Berries, which can be eaten to recover HP. Any HP recovered from an OranBerry eaten within 5s that exceeds user's max HP will be converted to a shield. If the user catches sight of a Berry while using this move, movement speed is increased for a short time. At level 11, this move drops more Berries.

Title: No Retreat (move)

No Retreat (Japanese: はいすいのじん Last Stand) is a non-damaging Fighting-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Falinks. No Retreat raises the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed by one stage each. It also prevents the user from switching out or fleeing. No Retreat fails if the user has been prevented from switching out or fleeing due to using No Retreat. However, if the user has already been given the "Can't Escape" status by another move such as Mean Look, No Retreat will successfully grant its stat boosts but will not create an additional status line to trap the user again. In this case, using No Retreat multiple times will create multiple boosts. The user of No Retreat can still switch out after using the move if it is holding a Shed Shell, if its Eject Button activates, if its opponent's Red Card activates, or if its opponent uses a move on it that forces the target to switch out, such as Whirlwind or Dragon Tail. If the user is Ghost-type, they are also free to switch, but the Pokémon will still receive a "Can't Escape" status on its status screen, which will prevent it from using No Retreat again. No Retreat has the highest cooldown of all moves in Pokémon Legends: Z-A.

Title: Tar Shot (move)

Tar Shot (Japanese: タールショット Tar Shot) is a non-damaging Rock-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Coalossal. Tar Shot lowers the target's Speed stat by one stage and doubles the effectiveness of Fire-type moves used on the target. Tar Shot will not double the effectiveness of Fire-type moves used on a target that is already under the effect of Tar Shot (but may still lower its Speed). The increased vulnerability to Fire-type attacks does not affect a Pokémon that is Terastallized, however if a Pokémon already affected by the increased vulnerability Terastallizes, the effect will remain.

Title: Magic Powder (move)

Magic Powder (Japanese: まほうのこな Magic Powder) is a non-damaging Psychic-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Hatterene. Magic Powder changes the target's type to pure Psychic. The effect lasts as long as the target remains in battle; however, changing form, transforming, or Terastallizing overrides the effect. Magic Powder fails if the target has a substitute, is already a pure Psychic type, is Terastallized, or its Ability is RKS System. Being a powder move, it fails if the target is a Grass-type Pokémon, has the Ability Overcoat, or is holding Safety Goggles. Magic Powder is the only powder-based move that cannot be learned by a Grass or Bug-type Pokémon.

Title: Dragon Darts (move)

Dragon Darts (Japanese: ドラゴンアロー Dragon Arrow) is a damage-dealing Dragon-type multistrike move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Dragapult. Dragon Darts is a multistrike move that deals damage twice. If targeting an opponent when there are multiple opponents, Dragon Darts' first strike targets the original target and its second strike targets the other opponent. If either the original target or its ally would take no damage from Dragon Darts, both strikes are directed at the other Pokémon instead. If Dragon Darts targets an ally, both hits will hit that ally. Wide Guard does not affect Dragon Darts. The following reasons for taking no damage from Dragon Darts will cause it to target the other Pokémon: If Dragon Darts would miss due to the user's accuracy or the target's evasion If Dragon Darts would miss due to targeting a Tatsugiri which is in its ally Dondozo's mouth If both target Pokémon are immune to Dragon Darts, only one of their immunity is acknowledged. If one of Dragon Darts' strikes is redirected in order to avert a miss due to accuracy and evasion, this will activate the user's Blunder Policy (even if both strikes successfully hit the other Pokémon). If an opponent is the center of attention (such as due to Follow Me), both strikes will target that Pokémon, even if it is immune to Dragon Darts. Pressure, will deduct additional PP for each Pokémon the move targets that has the ability. In a Max Raid Battle, if used by the wild Pokémon, each strike targets two opponents individually, hitting one Pokémon first and then another Pokémon adjacent to the first target. If the user has Prankster and calls Dragon Darts via a move that calls other moves, such as Copycat, and one of the targets is Dark-type, its type immunity is acknowledged properly and it hits the other target twice. If a Focus Sash activates before the last strike is dealt, the following strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can still activate repeatedly to prevent fainting, but each chance is independent. Sturdy will activate with each hit if the defending Pokémon has a maximum HP of 1. In Pokémon UNITE, Dragapult's Unite Move, Dreep and Destroy, is similar to Dragon Darts. Dragon Darts has some unique animation traits in Generation VIII. The move's animation is different if: The move is used by a Pokémon that is not Dragapult. A Shiny Pokémon uses Dragon Darts, with the Dreepy also appearing as Shiny. It shares this distinction with Mind Blown and Syrup Bomb. Due to the change in the move's animation going into Generation IX, it is no longer possible to see either of these differences.

Title: Teatime (move)

Teatime (Japanese: おちゃかい Tea Party) is a non-damaging Normal-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Polteageist. Teatime causes all Pokémon on the field to eat their held Berry. This move bypasses substitutes. If a Pokémon has eaten its Berry, Teatime fails to work on that Pokémon. The Berry is eaten even if it would not have any effect, such as eating a Mago Berry at full health. If no Pokémon on the field has a held Berry, Teatime fails with the message "But nothing happened!". Teatime causes Pokémon to eat their Berries even in the presence of Unnerve or Magic Room. This move does not affect Pokémon under the effect of Max Guard. Pokémon in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move are not affected by Teatime. If Teatime has its type changed by Electrify or Plasma Fists, Pokémon with Volt Absorb, Lightning Rod, or Motor Drive will not use up their Berries but will instead gain the benefit of the Ability regardless of whether they are holding a Berry or not.

Title: Octolock (move)

Octolock (Japanese: たこがため Octopus Hold) is a non-damaging Fighting-type move introduced in Generation VIII. Prior to Generation IX, it was the signature move of Grapploct. Octolock prevents the target from fleeing or switching out. At the end of each turn, the target's Defense and Special Defense are lowered by one stage each. If the user switches out, the target will be freed.

Title: Bolt Beak (move)

Bolt Beak (Japanese: でんげきくちばし Electrical Shock Beak) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of the Fossilized Bird Pokémon. Bolt Beak deals damage. If the user attacks before the target, or if the target switches in during the turn that Bolt Beak is used, its base power doubles to 170.

Title: Fishious Rend (move)

Fishious Rend (Japanese: エラがみ Gill Bite) is a damage-dealing Water-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of the Fossilized Fish Pokémon. Fishious Rend deals damage. If the user attacks before the target, or if the target switches in during the turn that Fishious Rend is used, its base power doubles to 170. Strong Jaw further increases power by 50%. Despite being considered a biting move, its description claims that the Pokémon uses its gills to use the attack.

Title: Court Change (move)

Court Change (Japanese: コートチェンジ Court Change) is a non-damaging Normal-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is one of the signature moves of Cinderace. Court Change causes any active battle effects to swap sides. This affects barriers like Reflect, multi-turn effects like Tailwind, and entry hazards like Spikes. Court Change does not cause any Pokémon afflicted with a volatile or non-volatile status condition to pass that condition to another Pokémon, nor does it transfer any Pokémon's stat changes to any other Pokémon. Court Change has no effect on effects that affect the entire field (like weather, terrain, or Trick Room). If used on an effect created by a G-Max Move, such as G-Max Volcalith, Court Change will successfully switch the passive effect so long as it only impacts one side of the field. If both sides of the battle are affected by the same effect, but are each on different remaining turns, Court Change will swap both effects' remaining turns. If Court Change transfers an entry hazard, it will not have any effect until a new Pokémon is switched in. If a Sticky Web is swapped back to its user's side of the field, and a Pokémon with Defiant or Competitive is then switched onto this Sticky Web, the Abilities will not activate. Defiant or Competitive will now activate because of a Sticky Web swapped back to its user's side of the field.

Title: Max Flare (move)

Max Flare (Japanese: ダイバーン Dai Burn) is a damage-dealing Fire-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Flare inflicts damage and creates harsh sunlight for 5 turns (8 if a Heat Rock is held). Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Flare if it knows a damaging Fire-type move.

Title: Max Flutterby (move)

Max Flutterby (Japanese: ダイワーム Dai Worm) is a damage-dealing Bug-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Flutterby inflicts damage and then decreases the target's and its ally's Special Attack by one stage. Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Flutterby if it knows a damaging Bug-type move.

Title: Max Lightning (move)

Max Lightning (Japanese: ダイサンダー Dai Thunder) is a damage-dealing Electric-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Lightning inflicts damage and causes the battlefield to become Electric Terrain for 5 turns (8 if a Terrain Extender is held). Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Lightning if it knows a damaging Electric-type move. Description prior to Version 2.21.0 (since May 26, 2022): Turns the field into Electric Terrain.

Title: Max Strike (move)

Max Strike (Japanese: ダイアタック Dai Attack) is a damage-dealing Normal-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Strike does damage to a target and lowers the Speed of all opponents by one stage. Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. If Max Strike is used with Multi-Attack or Techno Blast as its base move while the user is holding a Memory or a Drive, respectively, Max Strike turns into the Max Move of the type corresponding to the type of Memory or Drive held by the user. If Max Strike is used during weather with Weather Ball as its base move, it turns into the Max Move of the type corresponding to the type Weather Ball would be in that weather. In a similar way, if Max Strike is used during terrain with Terrain Pulse as its base move, it turns into the Max Move of the type corresponding to the type Terrain Pulse would be in that terrain. If Max Strike is used by a Pokémon with Aerilate, Galvanize, Pixilate, or Refrigerate, it turns into the Max Move of the type corresponding to the Pokémon's Ability. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Strike if it knows a damaging Normal-type move.

Title: Max Knuckle (move)

Max Knuckle (Japanese: ダイナックル Dai Knuckle) is a damage-dealing Fighting-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Knuckle inflicts damage and increases the Attack of the user and its allies by one stage. Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Compared to other Max Moves, it usually has less power. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Knuckle if it knows a damaging Fighting-type move.

Title: Max Phantasm (move)

Max Phantasm (Japanese: ダイホロウ Dai Hollow) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Phantasm inflicts damage and lowers the target's and its ally's Defense by one stage. Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Phantasm if it knows a damaging Ghost-type move.

Title: Max Hailstorm (move)

Max Hailstorm (Japanese: ダイアイス Dai Ice) is a damage-dealing Ice-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Hailstorm inflicts damage and creates hail for 5 turns (8 if an Icy Rock is held). Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Hailstorm if it knows a damaging Ice-type move.

Title: Max Ooze (move)

Max Ooze (Japanese: ダイアシッド Dai Acid) is a damage-dealing Poison-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Ooze inflicts damage and then raises the user's and its ally's Special Attack by one stage. Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Compared to the other Max Moves, it usually has less power. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Ooze if it knows a damaging Poison-type move.

Title: Max Geyser (move)

Max Geyser (Japanese: ダイストリーム Dai Stream) is a damage-dealing Water-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Geyser inflicts damage and creates rain for 5 turns (8 if a Damp Rock is held). Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Geyser if it knows a damaging Water-type move. Max Geyser was the first Max Move and Generation VIII move seen in the animated series.

Title: Max Airstream (move)

Max Airstream (Japanese: ダイジェット Dai Jet) is a damage-dealing Flying-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Airstream inflicts damage and raises the user's and its ally's Speed by one stage. Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Airstream if it knows a damaging Flying-type move.

Title: Max Starfall (move)

Max Starfall (Japanese: ダイフェアリー Dai Fairy) is a damage-dealing Fairy-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Starfall inflicts damage and causes the battlefield to become Misty Terrain for 5 turns (8 if a Terrain Extender is held). Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Starfall if it knows a damaging Fairy-type move.

Title: Max Wyrmwind (move)

Max Wyrmwind (Japanese: ダイドラグーン Dai Dragoon) is a damage-dealing Dragon-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Wyrmwind inflicts damage and lowers the target's and its ally's Attack by one stage. Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Wyrmwind if it knows a damaging Dragon-type move.

Title: Max Mindstorm (move)

Max Mindstorm (Japanese: ダイサイコ Dai Psycho) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Mindstorm inflicts damage and causes the battlefield to become Psychic Terrain for 5 turns (8 if a Terrain Extender is held). Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Mindstorm if it knows a damaging Psychic-type move.

Title: Max Rockfall (move)

Max Rockfall (Japanese: ダイロック Dai Rock) is a damage-dealing Rock-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Rockfall inflicts damage and creates a sandstorm for 5 turns (8 if a Smooth Rock is held). Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Rockfall if it knows a damaging Rock-type move. Max Rockfall hits all opponents when used by Rose & Copperajah due to their passive skill P-Moves & Max Moves Expansion.

Title: Max Quake (move)

Max Quake (Japanese: ダイアース Dai Earth) is a damage-dealing Ground-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Quake inflicts damage and raises the user's and its ally's Special Defense by one stage. Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Quake if it knows a damaging Ground-type move. Max Quake hits all opponents when used by Victor & Rillaboom or Rose & Copperajah due to their passive skills Targets Maxed and P-Moves & Max Moves Expansion, respectively.

Title: Max Darkness (move)

Max Darkness (Japanese: ダイアーク Dai Evil) is a damage-dealing Dark-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Darkness inflicts damage and lowers the target's and its ally's Special Defense by one stage. Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Darkness if it knows a damaging Dark-type move.

Title: Max Overgrowth (move)

Max Overgrowth (Japanese: ダイソウゲン Dai Grassland) is a damage-dealing Grass-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Overgrowth inflicts damage and causes the battlefield to become Grassy Terrain for 5 turns (8 if a Terrain Extender is held). Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Overgrowth if it knows a damaging Grass-type move. In Early-Evening Waves, Milo's Eldegoss was revealed to be capable of using Max Overgrowth, but the move was used offscreen. Max Overgrowth's Japanese name is likely a pun on 大草原(だいそうげん) (prairie).

Title: Max Steelspike (move)

Max Steelspike (Japanese: ダイスチル Dai Steel) is a damage-dealing Steel-type Max Move introduced in Generation VIII. Max Steelspike inflicts damage and raises the user's and its ally's Defense by one stage. Its power and whether it is a special or physical move depends on the move it is based on. Any Dynamax Pokémon can use Max Steelspike if it knows a damaging Steel-type move.

Title: Clangorous Soul (move)

Clangorous Soul (Japanese: ソウルビート Soul Beat) is a non-damaging Dragon-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is one of the signature moves of Kommo-o. Clangorous Soul raises the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense and Speed by one stage each, at the cost of 1/3 of its maximum HP. If all of the user's stats mentioned above are at +6 stages, or its remaining HP is less than or equal to 1/3 of its maximum, Clangorous Soul fails. Though Z-Moves have been removed from the core series as of Generation VIII, Clangorous Soul could be seen as a downgraded version of Kommo-o's signature Z-Move, Clangorous Soulblaze; the moves' names are similar in all languages, and both moves increase the user's stats the same way, though Clangorous Soulblaze requires no HP decrease, does damage to the opponent in addition to the stat increase it provides, and can only be used once per battle.

Title: Body Press (move)

Body Press (Japanese: ボディプレス Body Press) is a damage-dealing Fighting-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It was TR99 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and is TM089 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Body Press inflicts damage, but it uses the user's Defense stat instead of its Attack stat to calculate damage. The user's Defense stat stage-modifiers are applied (rather than Attack stage-modifiers), but otherwise its Attack modifiers are used (including held item, Ability, and burn). For example, the user's Huge Power or a held Choice Band will increase the stat used in calculation, whereas Fur Coat or a held Eviolite will not. In the same vein, Slow Start or Defeatist can decrease the stat used in calculation, whereas Sword of Ruin cannot. Along with Life Dew, Body Press is one of the only Generation VIII moves to be usable in Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

Title: Decorate (move)

Decorate (Japanese: デコレーション Decoration) is a non-damaging Fairy-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Alcremie. Decorate raises the target's Attack and Special Attack stats by two stages each. Unlike most moves, it bypasses moves like Protect, Detect, and even Max Guard to affect the target but is blocked by Crafty Shield. It cannot affect a target that has a substitute or in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move. In Versions 1.0 to 2.0.2 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Decorate could not be selected in a battle. Starting with Version 3.0.0, it is usable and functions the same as it did in the previous generation.

Title: Drum Beating (move)

Drum Beating (Japanese: ドラムアタック Drum Attack) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Rillaboom. Drum Beating inflicts damage and lowers the target's Speed stat by one stage. In Pokémon GO, Drum Beating is a Charged Attack that became available starting January 18, 2026.

Title: Snap Trap (move)

Snap Trap (Japanese: トラバサミ Foothold Trap) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Galarian Stunfisk. In addition to dealing damage, Snap Trap inflicts 1/8 of the target's maximum HP as damage per turn for four to five turns upon use. It also traps the target, preventing switching and escape. If the user switches out, the target will be freed. If the user of Snap Trap is holding a Grip Claw, the duration will always be 7 turns. If a Binding Band is held by the user, the damage done at the end of each turn will increase from 1/8 of the target's maximum HP to 1/6.

Title: Pyro Ball (move)

Pyro Ball (Japanese: かえんボール Flame Ball) is a damage-dealing Fire-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is one of the signature moves of Cinderace. Pyro Ball will thaw out the user if it is frozen, and then inflict damage on the target. Pyro Ball also has a 10% chance of burning the target. It has no effect on Pokémon with the Bulletproof Ability. In Pokémon GO, Pyro Ball is a Charged Attack that became available starting March 14, 2026. In Pokémon UNITE, Pyro Ball is Cinderace's first move. It is obtained by evolving from Raboot at level 7 and upgrading Ember into it instead of Blaze Kick. The user kicks a ball of flame in a designated direction, burning and damaging enemies hit. At level 11, after this move hits, the cooldown on user's other move is decreased. When their sync move is Fire-type, Gloria (Alt. 2) & Cinderace's passive skill Fuel Economy 2 makes this move cost two move gauge slots to use. When their sync move is Fighting-type, Gloria (Alt. 2) & Cinderace's passive skill Piercing Gaze makes their use of this move never miss.

Title: Behemoth Blade (move)

Behemoth Blade (Japanese: きょじゅうざん Behemoth Slash) is a damage-dealing Steel-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Zacian. Behemoth Blade deals damage. If the target is Dynamaxed or Gigantamaxed, its damage is doubled. The bonus does not apply to Eternamax Eternatus, despite its similarities to a Gigantamaxed Pokémon. As there is no Dynamax mechanic in Generation IX, Behemoth Blade now deals damage with no secondary effect. If Zacian knows Iron Head while entering battle in its Crowned Sword form, Iron Head will become Behemoth Blade for the duration of the battle. In Pokémon GO, Behemoth Blade is a Charged Attack that debuted on May 29, 2025. It replaces Iron Head when Zacian transforms from its Hero of Many Battles form into its Crowned Sword form. Behemoth Blade can also be used in Max Battles as a Max Move, and can be leveled up as one. Behemoth Blade can't be replaced with another move using Charged TMs. Its Adventure Effect outside of battle, which became available on June 24, 2025, will increase the power of the attacks used by the player's Pokémon by 10% during Raids and by 5% during Max Battles. It costs 5,000 Stardust and 5 Zacian Candy, and lasts 6 minutes. Description prior to Version 2.20.0 (released on April 27, 2022): This move's power increases 50% when the target has a sync buff. This attack targets all opponents and always results in a critical hit when used by Hop & Zacian due to their passive skill The Sword That Saved Galar. This attack targets all opponents and always results in a critical hit due to Hop & Zacian's passive skill The Sword That Saved Galar. Description of 6★ EX effect prior to Version 2.18.5 (released on March 15, 2022): Additional Effects: Sync move targets all opponents. Description prior to Version 2.20.0 (released on April 27, 2022): This move's power increases when the target has a sync buff. In Pokémon UNITE, Zacian's Unite Move, Sovereign Sword, is similar to Behemoth Blade. If used by a transformed Pokémon, Behemoth Blade will display a shorter animation with the transformed Pokémon stuck in a default pose. Behemoth Blade and Behemoth Bash were formerly not present in the mobile version of Pokémon HOME's move Pokédex, as they are the signature moves of Crowned Sword Zacian and Crowned Shield Zamazenta (which are forms that appear only in battles and, therefore, cannot be deposited into HOME) and cannot be learned by a Smeargle via Sketch, as it is not present in Pokémon Sword and Shield. With the addition of Smeargle to Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, the two moves were finally added to Pokémon HOME. This distinction is shared with Glacial Lance and Astral Barrage for the same reasoning, as well as Struggle which cannot be legitimately learned by any Pokémon, and Light of Ruin as Pokémon Legends: Z-A is not yet compatible with Pokémon HOME.

Title: Behemoth Bash (move)

Behemoth Bash (Japanese: きょじゅうだん Behemoth Impact) is a damage-dealing Steel-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Zamazenta. Behemoth Bash deals damage. If the target is Dynamaxed or Gigantamaxed, its damage is doubled. The bonus does not apply to Eternamax Eternatus, despite its similarities to a Gigantamaxed Pokémon. As there is no Dynamax mechanic in Generation IX, Behemoth Bash now deals damage with no secondary effect. If Zamazenta knows Iron Head while entering battle in its Crowned Shield form, Iron Head will become Behemoth Bash for the duration of the battle. In Pokémon GO, Behemoth Bash is a Charged Attack that debuted on May 29, 2025. It replaces Iron Head when Zamazenta transforms from its Hero of Many Battles form into its Crowned Shield form. Behemoth Bash can also be used in Max Battles as a Max Move, and can be leveled up as one. Behemoth Bash can't be replaced with another move using Charged TMs. Its Adventure Effect outside of battle, which became available on June 24, 2025, will reduce the damage taken by Pokémon by 10% during Raids and by 5% during Max Battles. It costs 5,000 Stardust and 5 Zamazenta Candy, and lasts 6 minutes. Description prior to Version 2.20.0 (released on April 27, 2022): This move's power increases 50% when the target has a sync buff. This sync move could not be raised to 6★ EX until July 14, 2023—1 year, 11 months, 2 days after its release date (on August 12, 2021). Description prior to Version 2.20.0 (released on April 27, 2022): This move's power increases when the target has a sync buff. Behemoth Bash and Behemoth Blade were formerly not present in the mobile version of Pokémon HOME's move Pokédex, as they are the signature moves of Crowned Shield Zamazenta and Crowned Sword Zacian (which are forms that appear only in battles and, therefore, cannot be deposited into HOME) and cannot be learned by a Smeargle via Sketch, as it is not present in Pokémon Sword and Shield. With the addition of Smeargle to Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, the two moves were finally added to Pokémon HOME. This distinction is shared with Glacial Lance and Astral Barrage for the same reasoning, as well as Struggle and Light of Ruin, both of which cannot be legitimately learned by any Pokémon.

Title: Aura Wheel (move)

Aura Wheel (Japanese: オーラぐるま Aura Wheel) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Morpeko. Aura Wheel inflicts damage and raises the user's Speed stat by one stage. Its type changes depending on Morpeko's current mode. The user's Speed stat will not be raised if the attack misses or the target is immune to or protected from the move. Aura Wheel can only be successfully used by Morpeko (or a Pokémon that has transformed into Morpeko). If this move is used by any other Pokémon, such as through Mimic, the move will fail, displaying the battle text "But <Pokémon> can't use the move!" Morpeko will now change forms every time it uses Aura Wheel. Description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021): Raises the user's Speed. When the user is in Full Belly Mode, this attack becomes Electric-type. When the user is in Hangry Mode, this attack becomes Dark-type. Description prior to Version 2.23.0 (from July 27, 2022): Raises the user's Speed by one stat rank. When the user is in Full Belly Mode, this attack becomes Electric-type. When the user is in Hangry Mode, this attack becomes Dark-type. Aura Wheel's type will change to Dark when used by Morpeko in its Battle-Only Form, Hangry Mode. This move's animation is a reference to hamster wheels. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Aura Wheel is the only move that can be copied by Sketch but not be used by Smeargle.

Title: Breaking Swipe (move)

Breaking Swipe (Japanese: ワイドブレイカー Wide Breaker) is a damage-dealing Dragon-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It was TM99 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and is TM222 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Breaking Swipe deals damage to all opponents. It lowers the Attack of all targets by one stage. Stat decreases caused by this move apply to each target hit. Iris (Alt.) & Hydreigon's passive skill Piercing Gaze makes their use of this move never miss. Description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021): Lowers the target's Attack. This buddy move was known as B Breaking Swipe prior to version 2.38.0. In Pokémon GO, Breaking Swipe is a Charged Attack that was made available on February 22, 2023. Breaking Swipe is the only Dragon-type move that can be taught by TM in Pokémon Sword and Shield.

Title: Branch Poke (move)

Branch Poke (Japanese: えだづき Branch Poke) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced in Generation VIII. Branch Poke deals damage and has no secondary effect. In Pokémon UNITE, Branch Poke is Trevenant's second move. The user jabs in the designated direction, dealing damage to enemies and decreasing their movement speed by 30% for 2s when it hits. At level 7, this move is replaced by either Horn Leech or Pain Split.

Title: Overdrive (move)

Overdrive redirects here. For the modes of Reshiram, Zekrom, and Kyurem, see List of Pokémon with form differences → Reshiram, Zekrom, and Kyurem. Overdrive (Japanese: オーバードライブ Overdrive) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Toxtricity. Overdrive is a sound-based move. Overdrive can hit Pokémon even if they are behind a substitute. Overdrive is the only Electric-type sound-based move.

Title: Apple Acid (move)

Apple Acid (Japanese: りんごさん Malic Acid) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Appletun. Apple Acid inflicts damage and lowers the target's Special Defense stat by one stage. Apple Acid is the only signature move of one of Applin's evolutions to not have an effect exclusive to it.

Title: Grav Apple (move)

Grav Apple (Japanese: Ｇのちから G-Force) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Flapple. Grav Apple inflicts damage and lowers the target's Defense stat by one stage. If Gravity is in effect, Grav Apple's base power increases to 120. The move is likely based on the popular story of how Isaac Newton came up with his theory of gravity when he saw an apple fall from a tree. The move's name is also a pun on gravity and crab apple, a small type of apple that resembles a cherry.

Title: Spirit Break (move)

Spirit Break (Japanese: ソウルクラッシュ Soul Crash) is a damage-dealing Fairy-type move introduced in Generation VIII. Prior to Generation IX, it was one of the signature moves of Grimmsnarl. Spirit Break inflicts damage and lowers the target's Special Attack stat by one stage.

Title: Strange Steam (move)

Strange Steam (Japanese: ワンダースチーム Wonder Steam) is a damage-dealing Fairy-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Galarian Weezing. Strange Steam deals damage and has a 20% chance of confusing the target.

Title: Life Dew (move)

Life Dew (Japanese: いのちのしずく Life Droplets) is a non-damaging Water-type move introduced in Generation VIII. Life Dew restores the HP of the user and its allies by 25% of their maximum HP. If an ally has Water Absorb, Storm Drain, or Dry Skin, it will activate and negate this move's effect on it. It affects allies with substitutes but cannot affect a Pokémon in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move. This move originated as an ACE SPEC Item card in the TCG. Along with Body Press, Life Dew is one of the only Generation VIII moves to be usable in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

Title: Obstruct (move)

Obstruct (Japanese: ブロッキング Blocking) is a non-damaging Dark-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Obstagoon. Obstruct protects the user from all effects of physical and special moves that target it during the turn it is used. If Obstruct blocks an attack that would make contact with the user, the attacker's Defense stat is lowered by 2 stages. Obstruct causes the Defense stat to drop even if the user of Obstruct was immune to that contact move. (Moves that are blocked do not activate other effects that require making contact, like Rocky Helmet or Rough Skin.) If the user goes last in the turn, the move will fail. The chance that Obstruct will succeed also drops each time the user successfully and consecutively uses Endure, any protection move that only affects the user, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard. Each time, the chance of success is divided by 3. Some moves will do damage through Obstruct. Most damaging Max Moves will only do 25% of their original damage, except G-Max One Blow and G-Max Rapid Flow which do full damage. Moves that break protection, like Feint, will do their full damage and remove the effects of Obstruct. The Ability Unseen Fist allows contact moves to bypass Obstruct. Obstruct is an increased priority move that will go before most attacks in a turn. Description prior to version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021): ...If the move was a physical attack move, sharply lowers the Defense of the opponent that used it. In Pokémon GO, Obstruct is a Charged Attack that was made available on August 13, 2022. Obstagoon's Pokémon Sword Pokédex entry states that shouting is part of this move, and the user is seen shouting in the move animation. Despite this, Obstruct is not a sound-based move.

Title: False Surrender (move)

False Surrender (Japanese: どげざつき Groveling Strike) is a damage-dealing Dark-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Morgrem and Grimmsnarl. False Surrender inflicts damage and bypasses accuracy checks to always hit, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly.

Title: Meteor Assault (move)

Meteor Assault (Japanese: スターアサルト Star Assault) is a damage-dealing Fighting-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is the signature move of Sirfetch'd. Meteor Assault inflicts damage and then forces the user to recharge during the next turn. Meteor Assault is tied with Focus Punch for having the highest base power of all Fighting-type moves, excluding Z-Moves and Reversal at its full power.

Title: Eternabeam (move)

Eternabeam (Japanese: ムゲンダイビーム Infinity Beam) is a damage-dealing Dragon-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is one of the signature moves of Eternatus. Eternabeam inflicts damage and then forces the user to recharge during the next turn, unless the move fails due to missing, type immunity, or being blocked by a protection move. Eternabeam is the first instance of a Legendary Pokémon's signature move being removed from the game. When Eternatus uses Eternabeam, it briefly appears in its Eternamax form during the move's animation. This is the only time Eternamax Eternatus is seen outside of the battle against it during the climax of Pokémon Sword and Shield. Despite displaying the Eternamax form for the duration of the move's animation, the game does not treat this as an actual form change internally; Eternatus's stats and form index value remain unchanged, and Eternamax Eternatus will not be registered in the player's Pokédex upon using this move. Eternabeam is the only move that is a regular move in the core series games but is a max move in Pokémon Masters EX. This move's name includes a reference to Eternamax in all languages. In English, Japanese, Chinese, and Korean, it is also a reference to Eternatus.

Title: Steel Beam (move)

Steel Beam (Japanese: てっていこうせん Beam of Steely Resolve) is a damage-dealing Steel-type move introduced in Generation VIII. It is TM170 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet and TM157 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Steel Beam inflicts damage, and the user takes damage equal to half of its maximum HP rounded up (unless it has Magic Guard). The user of Steel Beam will take damage even if the target is protected from it, if it hits a substitute, or if the move misses. If Steel Beam fails due to there being no targets (i.e. if all Pokémon in range fainted before the move was used), the user does not take damage. Steel Beam's base power is changed from 140 to 120. Steel Beam is a recoil move in this game. A Move Tutor will teach Steel Beam to any Steel-type Pokémon (except for Dusk Mane Necrozma), as well as both Zacian and Zamazenta. Arceus and Silvally can also learn the move regardless of their current type. The Move Tutor is: Several other sync pairs have sync moves that include the name Steel Beam. However, these moves' animations are identical to the generic Steel Sync Beam, and are not based on the move Steel Beam. Description prior to version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021): The user takes damage equal to half of its maximum HP. The damage is taken even if the attack fails. Steel Beam is the only Steel-type move that damages the user. In April 2019, prior to the release of Pokémon Sword and Shield, CoroCoro Comics May ran a contest where fans could name the strongest Steel-type move. Steel Beam's Japanese name was the name that won this contest. The Japanese name of this move literally means an "all-out resistance" (徹底抗戦 tettei-kōsen), while also incorporating puns on 鉄 tetsu (iron or steel), 抵抗 teikō (resistance) and 光線 kōsen (beam). Not including Max Moves, Steel Beam was the first Generation VIII move seen in the animated series. It was used by Raihan's Duraludon in Toughing It Out!. Dusk Mane Necrozma is the only Steel-type Pokémon that cannot learn Steel Beam.

Title: Expanding Force (move)

Expanding Force (Japanese: ワイドフォース Wide Force) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM218 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Expanding Force inflicts damage. It normally targets a single Pokémon. If Psychic Terrain is in effect and the user is grounded upon its execution, this move gets a 1.5× power increase (in addition to the usual 1.3× bonus from terrain) and it becomes a move that targets both opponents. Alongside Scorching Sands, Expanding Force was the final standard move to debut in Pokémon the Series.

Title: Steel Roller (move)

Steel Roller (Japanese: アイアンローラー Iron Roller) is a damage-dealing Steel-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. In Generation IX, it is the signature move of Iron Treads. Steel Roller deals damage and removes any active terrain. If no terrain is active upon the move's execution, it will fail. Steel Roller has a unique animation if it is used by a Starmobile. It shares this trait with Spin Out.

Title: Scale Shot (move)
