Scale Shot (Japanese: スケイルショット Scale Shot) is a damage-dealing Dragon-type multistrike move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM200 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet and TM143 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Scale Shot inflicts damage, hitting the target 2-5 times per use. If the user does not miss, the user's Speed is raised by one stage, while its Defense is lowered by one stage. Each strike made has an equal chance to be a critical hit. There is a 35% chance that it will hit 2 times, a 35% chance it will hit 3 times, a 15% chance it will hit 4 times, and a 15% chance it will hit 5 times. The average total base power (including the 10% base miss chance) is 69.8. Scale Shot may continue attacking after breaking a substitute. Counter will only acknowledge the last strike of this move. If the user is holding a King's Rock or Razor Fang, each consecutive hit has an equal chance to cause the opponent to flinch. Pokémon with the Ability Skill Link will always hit five times when using Scale Shot unless it misses. If a Focus Sash activates before the last strike is dealt, the following strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can still activate repeatedly to prevent fainting, but each chance is independent. Sturdy will activate with each hit if the defending Pokémon has a maximum HP of 1. If the target has Weak Armor or Stamina, each strike will activate it. Scale Shot is not affected by Sheer Force.

Title: Meteor Beam (move)

Meteor Beam (Japanese: メテオビーム Meteor Beam) is a damage-dealing Rock-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM220 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet and TM156 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. On the turn it is selected, Meteor Beam will raise the user's Special Attack by one stage, and the message "[User] is overflowing with space power!" will be shown. On the following turn, Meteor Beam will do damage (factoring in the Special Attack boost), PP will be deducted from it, and it will count as the last move used. Once Meteor Beam is selected, the user won't be able to switch out until it is disrupted or fully executed. Sleep, freezing, binding, and flinching will pause but not disrupt the duration of Meteor Beam. If a Power Herb is held by the user, Meteor Beam can be executed in one turn. The user will charge and boost their Special Attack, consume the Power Herb, then execute the final stage of the move in the same turn. The Special Attack boost is applied because the charging stage is still performed. In Pokémon GO, Meteor Beam is a Charged Attack that was made available on September 18, 2022. Selene (Alt.) & Nihilego's passive skill Alola to You! makes this move lower the Sp. Def of each target by two stat ranks instead of one when the target is poisoned or badly poisoned. The power of this move scales for each Sp. Def drop on the target, increasing by 5% for each stat rank dropped, for a maximum power-up of 30%. Meteor Beam is learned by most extraterrestrial Pokémon and all Fossil Pokémon that are obtainable in Pokémon Sword and Shield.

Title: Shell Side Arm (move)

Shell Side Arm (Japanese: シェルアームズ Shell Arms) is a damage-dealing Poison-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is the signature move of Galarian Slowbro. Shell Side Arm deals damage and has a 20% chance of poisoning the target. Shell Side Arm forecasts whether its base damage would be higher as a physical move or special move, and becomes the category that it predicts would inflict more damage (chosen randomly if they are tied). For the forecast only, stat modifiers other than stat stages are ignored, and Wonder Room is applied to stat stage modifiers but not to the original stats. Shell Side Arm has a different animation depending on whether it is a physical or a special move. If it is a physical move, it makes contact; if it is a special move, it does not.

Title: Misty Explosion (move)

Misty Explosion (Japanese: ミストバースト Mist Burst) is a damage-dealing Fairy-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM201 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Misty Explosion inflicts damage and causes the user to faint. Under Misty Terrain, if the user is on the ground, Misty Explosion's base power is raised by 1.5×, making it 150. Misty Explosion fails without causing the user to faint if a Pokémon with the Ability Damp is on the field. If used in a Max or Tera Raid Battle, Misty Explosion fails, and the user does not faint. Self-Destruct, Explosion, and Misty Explosion are the only three damaging moves whose battle animations play out even if they would miss or have no effect.

Title: Grassy Glide (move)

Grassy Glide (Japanese: グラススライダー Grass Slider) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM194 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Grassy Glide inflicts damage. If Grassy Terrain is in effect and the user is grounded upon its execution, this move's priority is increased by one. In Version 2.0.1 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Grassy Glide's power was further reduced to 55. In Pokémon UNITE, Grassy Glide is Tsareena's second move. It is obtained by evolving from Steenee at level 6 and upgrading Rapid Spin into it instead of Trop Kick. The user slides in the designated direction, dealing damage to enemies hit. This is followed by an attack in the opposite direction, dealing damage and shoving enemies hit. If Queenly Majesty is active, this move grants the user a shield. At level 12, the move's damage is increased. Grassy Glide is the only move whose priority can vary because of its own effect. In Version 2.0.1 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Grassy Glide's base power was reduced from 60 to 55, making it the only move whose base power was changed via a patch to a game.

Title: Rising Voltage (move)

Rising Voltage (Japanese: ライジングボルト Rising Volt) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. In Generation IX, it is one of the signature moves of Raging Bolt. Rising Voltage deals damage. If the target is affected by Electric Terrain upon the move's execution, Rising Voltage's base power is doubled to 140.

Title: Terrain Pulse (move)

Terrain Pulse (Japanese: だいちのはどう Terrain Pulse) is a damage-dealing Normal-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. In Generation IX, it is the signature move of Smoliv's evolutionary line. Terrain Pulse deals damage. When there is no terrain active, Terrain Pulse is a Normal-type move. If the user is grounded under any type of terrain, Terrain Pulse's power doubles to 100 (type power-ups from the terrain will be put into effect in addition to this boost). Terrain Pulse's type and animation changes depending on the terrain at the time it is used. Electric-type if the battlefield is Electric Terrain. Psychic-type if the battlefield is Psychic Terrain. Terrain Pulse's power is increased by 50% when used by Pokémon with the Ability Mega Launcher. Terrain Pulse is unaffected by Normalize, Pixilate, Refrigerate, and Galvanize. Terrain Pulse is affected by Electrify. This causes Terrain Pulse to deal Electric damage, regardless of the terrain present. Its power will still be doubled to 100 if used in terrain. When used as a Max Move, Terrain Pulse will be displayed as Max Strike, but will change into the appropriately typed Max Move or G-Max Move upon execution, depending on the present terrain and whether or not the user is grounded when the move is actually executed.

Title: Skitter Smack (move)

Skitter Smack (Japanese: はいよるいちげき Creeping Strike) is a damage-dealing Bug-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM219 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Skitter Smack inflicts damage and lowers the target's Special Attack stat by one stage.

Title: Burning Jealousy (move)

Burning Jealousy (Japanese: しっとのほのお Flames of Jealousy) is a damage-dealing Fire-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM195 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Burning Jealousy inflicts damage to all adjacent opponents and will burn any opponent that has increased its stats during that turn. It ignores stat increases from Imposter when determining whether to burn an opponent. If the target is weak to Burning Jealousy and is holding a Weakness Policy, determining whether to burn an opponent happens before consuming the Weakness Policy. If the user has Sheer Force, then the power boost is applied regardless of whether it would have burnt the target or not.

Title: Lash Out (move)

Lash Out (Japanese: うっぷんばらし Vent Anger) is a damage-dealing Dark-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM199 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Lash Out deals damage. If any of the user's stats were lowered during the turn this move is used, its base power is doubled to 150. Lash Out does not power up if stat stages are modified directly due to an effect such as Haze, Clear Smog, or Topsy-Turvy.

Title: Poltergeist (move)

Poltergeist (Japanese: ポルターガイスト Poltergeist) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM198 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Poltergeist deals damage if the target is holding an item, after displaying the message "[target] is about to be attacked by its [item]!" If used when the target is not holding an item, the move fails. If used on a Pokémon weak to Ghost-type moves holding a Kasib Berry or a Weakness Policy, or a Pokémon holding a Red Card, Poltergeist will still do damage after the item has been consumed, but the item in question still takes effect. In Pokémon GO, Poltergeist is a Charged Attack that was made available on October 15, 2022. Poltergeist is the only Ghost-type move with an accuracy lower than 100%.

Title: Corrosive Gas (move)

Corrosive Gas (Japanese: ふしょくガス Corrosive Gas) is a non-damaging Poison-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. Corrosive Gas removes the targets' held items for the rest of the battle (even if switched out). Held items removed by Corrosive Gas cannot be recovered by Recycle or Harvest. Corrosive Gas does not affect an item held by Pokémon with the Sticky Hold Ability, or if it hits a Pokémon's substitute. It also does not affect a Griseous Orb, Drive, Memory, Rusted Sword, or Rusted Shield held by Giratina, Genesect, Silvally, Zacian, or Zamazenta, respectively. As no available Pokémon can learn Corrosive Gas, it can only be used through Metronome. This move's effect is the same as the secondary effect of Knock Off from Generation V onwards. Corrosive Gas is the only Move Tutor move introduced in The Isle of Armor to not be learnable by any Pokémon in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Title: Coaching (move)

Coaching (Japanese: コーチング Coaching) is a non-damaging Fighting-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM213 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Coaching boosts the Attack and Defense of all allies (but not the user or allies in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move) by one stage each. It bypasses all forms of protection to affect the allies, including Crafty Shield. If used in a Single Battle or when no ally is present, Coaching will fail. In Pokémon UNITE, Coaching is one of Mew's possible second moves. It is obtained by reaching level 3 and choosing it over Light Screen and Agility. Coaching has the user move toward an ally Pokémon to grant it a shield and increase its basic attack speed. Each time Coaching is used, the cooldown of Mew's Electro Ball, Solar Beam, or Surf is reduced. Also, if Coaching is used on an ally Pokémon that hasn't been targeted by it for a set amount of time, the move's cooldown immediately resets. By reaching level 12, Coaching is upgraded into Coaching+, and its cooldown and the waiting time before using it again on a recently coached ally Pokémon without triggering its cooldown are reduced.

Title: Flip Turn (move)

Flip Turn (Japanese: クイックターン Quick Turn) is a damage-dealing Water-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM196 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet and TM008 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Flip Turn deals damage and then switches the user out. The user will not be switched out if the user is the only Pokémon in the party that is able to battle, if Flip Turn fails to hit the target, if Flip Turn triggers an opponent's Wimp Out or Emergency Exit, or if using Flip Turn ends the battle. Flip Turn will not switch the user out if the user is holding a Red Card or the target is holding an Eject Button. If the player's Pokémon knocks out an opposing Trainer's Pokémon by using Flip Turn, the replacement that the player sends out will not be considered to have battled for experience purposes, but if it holds an Amulet Coin or Luck Incense that item will be taken into account when calculating prize money. In switch-style battles, the player will still have the ability to switch Pokémon between opponent's Pokémon fainting. Flip Turn switches the user out even if it is under the effect of Ingrain, Arena Trap, Magnet Pull, Shadow Tag, Mean Look, or Block. A flip turn, also known as a tumble turn, is a turn technique in swimming that is performed during freestyle and backstroke races. Athletes use the flip turn to quickly and smoothly turn around in the water to change direction. Flip Turn's French name is a pun on eau (water) and au revoir (goodbye).

Title: Triple Axel (move)

Triple Axel (Japanese: トリプルアクセル Triple Axel) is a damage-dealing Ice-type multistrike move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM212 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet and TM144 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Triple Axel inflicts damage, hitting the target up to three times per use. Its base power will increase by 20 with each successive strike, so the first, second, and third strikes have powers of 20, 40, and 60, respectively. Each of Triple Axel's strikes has a separate accuracy check. If any strike fails, the move ends. Assuming no modifications to accuracy or evasion, since each strike has 90% base accuracy, there is a: 72.9% chance that it will hit 3 times (for cumulative 120 base power), 8.1% chance that it will hit twice (for cumulative 60 base power), 9% chance that it will hit once (for 20 base power), With all three accuracy checks and base powers taken into account, the move's average effective power is 94.14. If the target has an Ability that activates upon contact (such as Weak Armor or Stamina), each strike counts individually, enabling the Ability to activate multiple times. If the target is holding a Rocky Helmet, the user is damaged for each strike. If the user has Skill Link or is holding Loaded Dice, the move will have only one accuracy check. If the first hit misses due to Disguise, the first hit will count as a strike, but the power of the two subsequent hits will be 20 and 40 respectively. If a Focus Sash activates before the last strike is dealt, the following strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can still activate repeatedly to prevent fainting, but each chance is independent. Sturdy will activate with each hit if the defending Pokémon has a maximum HP of 1. This buddy move was known as B Triple Axel prior to version 2.38.0. In Pokémon UNITE, Triple Axel is Tsareena's first move. It is obtained by reaching level 5 as Steenee and upgrading Razor Leaf into it instead of Stomp. The user spin-kicks 3 times in the designated direction, dealing damage to enemies. Landed hits add a Triple Axel Buff stack. The stacks last for 5s and decay together. During the duration of the buff, the basic attack speed is increased depending upon the number of stacks (caps at 3 stacks). In addition, all damage dealt by basic attacks or Triple Axel during the buff is increased by 5% per stack (caps at 6 stacks). If Queenly Majesty is active, each kick heals the user. At level 10, this move's damage is increased. Along with Triple Kick and Population Bomb, it is one of only three multistrike moves where the chance to miss for successful hits is independent of the first hit. Triple Axel has the same description text as Triple Kick. Triple Axel may be based on a variation of the Axel jump with the same name in figure skating. Even though Triple Axel is referred to as a "consecutive three-kick attack", Seel, Dewgong, Bellossom, Milotic, Froslass, Cryogonal, the Popplio evolutionary line, Frosmoth, and Tatsugiri can all learn Triple Axel despite not having feet.

Title: Dual Wingbeat (move)

Dual Wingbeat (Japanese: ダブルウイング Double Wing) is a damage-dealing Flying-type multistrike move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM197 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet and TM134 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Dual Wingbeat inflicts damage, hitting twice per use. Each strike has an equal chance to be a critical hit. Dual Wingbeat will hit again if the first strike breaks a substitute. Counter will only acknowledge the second strike of this move. If the target has an Ability that activates upon contact or a Rocky Helmet, each strike counts individually, enabling the Ability or Rocky Helmet to activate twice. If a Focus Sash activates before the last strike is dealt, the following strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can still activate repeatedly to prevent fainting, but each chance is independent. Sturdy will activate with each hit if the defending Pokémon has a maximum HP of 1. If the user is holding a King's Rock or Razor Fang, each consecutive hit has an equal chance to cause the opponent to flinch. If the target has Weak Armor or Stamina, each strike will activate it. In Pokémon UNITE, Dual Wingbeat is one of Scyther's possible second moves. It is obtained by reaching level 5 and choosing to upgrade Fury Cutter into it instead of Bullet Punch. By doing so, Scyther will not evolve into Scizor. Dual Wingbeat has the user slash in front of itself with both of its scythes, dealing damage to opposing Pokémon in the X-shaped area of effect. If an opposing Pokémon is hit right in the center of the area of effect, the user's HP is restored. If Dual Wingbeat hits an opposing Pokémon, it can be used again within a set amount of time; doing so makes the user dash in the designated direction, dealing damage to opposing Pokémon it hits. The lower the opposing Pokémon's HP, higher will be the damage dealt by this dash. By reaching level 11, Dual Wingbeat is upgraded into Dual Wingbeat+, and the damage dealt by the move is increased. Dual Wingbeat is the only Flying-type multistrike move.

Title: Scorching Sands (move)

Scorching Sands (Japanese: ねっさのだいち Hot Sand Terrain) is a damage-dealing Ground-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is TM215 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Scorching Sands inflicts damage and has a 30% chance of burning the target. Upon execution, Scorching Sands will thaw the user if frozen (even if the move fails to hit the target). Scorching Sands also thaws a frozen target hit by it. Alongside Expanding Force, Scorching Sands was the final standard move to debut in Pokémon the Series.

Title: Jungle Healing (move)

Jungle Healing (Japanese: ジャングルヒール Jungle Heal) is a non-damaging Grass-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is the signature move of Zarude. Jungle Healing restores the HP of the user and its allies by 25% of their maximum HP, and heals any non-volatile status conditions they may have. In Secrets of the Jungle, Koko used Jungle Healing, marking the first time a human has used a Pokémon move in the animated series.

Title: Wicked Blow (move)

Wicked Blow (Japanese: あんこくきょうだ Dark Blow) is a damage-dealing Dark-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is the signature move of Single Strike Style Urshifu. Wicked Blow inflicts damage and will always result in a critical hit unless the target has Battle Armor or Shell Armor.

Title: Surging Strikes (move)

Surging Strikes (Japanese: すいりゅうれんだ Stream Strikes) is a damage-dealing Water-type multistrike move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Isle of Armor. It is the signature move of Rapid Strike Style Urshifu. Surging Strikes inflicts damage, hitting its target three times per use. Each strike will always result in a critical hit unless the target has Battle Armor or Shell Armor. If a Focus Sash activates before the last strike is dealt, the following strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can still activate repeatedly to prevent fainting, but each chance is independent. Sturdy will activate with each hit if the defending Pokémon has a maximum HP of 1. In Crackle!! Retrieve the Sword & Shield, Mustard's second Urshifu, which was soon after given to Henry and nicknamed Arman, was revealed to have learned Surging Strikes upon evolving.

Title: Thunder Cage (move)

Thunder Cage (Japanese: サンダープリズン Thunder Prison) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Crown Tundra. It is the signature move of Regieleki. In addition to dealing damage, Thunder Cage inflicts 1/8 of the target's maximum HP as damage per turn for four to five turns upon use. It also traps the target, preventing switching and escape. If the user switches out, the target will be freed. If the user of Thunder Cage is holding a Grip Claw, the duration will always be 7 turns. If a Binding Band is held by the user, the damage done at the end of each turn will increase from 1/8 of the target's maximum HP to 1/6. Thunder Cage most likely draws inspiration from 19th and 20th-century inventions on electromagnetism, such as the Tesla coil or the Faraday cage.

Title: Dragon Energy (move)

Dragon Energy (Japanese: ドラゴンエナジー Dragon Energy) is a damage-dealing Dragon-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Crown Tundra. It is the signature move of Regidrago. Dragon Energy deals damage. It has 150 base power at maximum HP, but its base power decreases proportionally to the user's remaining HP. Specifically, its base power is equal to ⌊150×HPcurrentHPmax⌋. However, if this formula produces a value less than 1, the move's base power becomes 1 instead. In a battle with multiple opponents, Dragon Energy will target all adjacent opponents.

Title: Freezing Glare (move)

Freezing Glare (Japanese: いてつくしせん Freezing Glare) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Crown Tundra. It is the signature move of Galarian Articuno. Freezing Glare inflicts damage and has a 10% chance to freeze the target. This buddy move was known as B Freezing Glare prior to version 2.38.0. Freezing Glare is the only Psychic-type move capable of freezing the target.

Title: Fiery Wrath (move)

Fiery Wrath (Japanese: もえあがるいかり Fiery Wrath) is a damage-dealing Dark-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Crown Tundra. It is the signature move of Galarian Moltres. Fiery Wrath damages all opponents. It has a 20% chance to cause each target to flinch. This buddy move was known as B Fiery Wrath prior to version 2.38.0.

Title: Thunderous Kick (move)

Thunderous Kick (Japanese: らいめいげり Thunderclap Kick) is a damage-dealing Fighting-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Crown Tundra. It is the signature move of Galarian Zapdos. Thunderous Kick inflicts damage and lowers the target's Defense stat. This buddy move was known as B Thunderous Kick prior to version 2.38.0.

Title: Glacial Lance (move)

Glacial Lance (Japanese: ブリザードランス Blizzard Lance) is a damage-dealing Ice-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Crown Tundra. It is the signature move of Ice Rider Calyrex. Glacial Lance deals damage to all adjacent opponents. In Generation VIII, when Glacial Lance is used by Ice Rider Calyrex, the animation is depicted as a spear being thrown at a chunk of ice. When used by other Pokémon, however, it is instead shown as a small beam of ice fired at the ice chunk. Glacial Lance can only be registered in the mobile version of Pokémon HOME's move Pokédex using a Smeargle that used Sketch to learn the move. This is possible in Pokemon Scarlet and Violet after The Indigo Disk DLC introduced Smeargle. It shares this trait with Astral Barrage, Behemoth Blade, Behemoth Bash, Ice Burn, and Freeze Shock.

Title: Astral Barrage (move)

Astral Barrage (Japanese: アストラルビット Astral Bit) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Crown Tundra. It is the signature move of Shadow Rider Calyrex. Astral Barrage deals damage to all adjacent opponents. Astral Barrage is tied with Shadow Force for the highest base power of all Ghost-type moves, excluding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. Astral Barrage can only be registered in the mobile version of Pokémon HOME's move Pokédex using a Smeargle that used Sketch to learn the move. This is possible in Pokemon Scarlet and Violet after The Indigo Disk DLC introduced Smeargle. It shares this trait with Glacial Lance, Behemoth Blade, Behemoth Bash, Ice Burn, and Freeze Shock.

Title: Eerie Spell (move)

Eerie Spell (Japanese: ぶきみなじゅもん Eerie Spell) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in The Crown Tundra. It is one of the signature moves of Galarian Slowking. Eerie Spell deals damage and causes 3 PP to be decreased from the last move the target used (if any) in the current battle. PP will not be deducted if the target faints from the move. Eerie Spell is a sound-based move. Eerie Spell can hit Pokémon even if they are behind a substitute.

Title: Dire Claw (move)

Dire Claw (Japanese: フェイタルクロー Fatal Claw) is a damage-dealing Poison-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Sneasler. Dire Claw inflicts damage and has an increased critical-hit ratio. It has a 50% chance (80% chance in the strong style) to either poison the target, paralyze it, or make it drowsy. Dire Claw's power has been changed from 60 to 80. It has a 50% chance of leaving the target poisoned, paralyzed, or asleep (approximately a 16.67% chance for each status individually), and does not have an increased critical-hit ratio. Dire Claw is the only Poison-type move that can inflict paralysis or sleep.

Title: Psyshield Bash (move)

Psyshield Bash (Japanese: バリアーラッシュ Barrier Rush) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Stantler and Wyrdeer. Psyshield Bash inflicts damage and raises the user's defensive stats. Psyshield Bash inflicts damage and raises the user's Defense by one stage. A Stantler that has used Psyshield Bash in the agile style at least 20 times can evolve into Wyrdeer. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Psyshield Bash's description says it "may raise the user's defensive stats", despite it in fact having a 100% chance to do so. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Psyshield Bash is the only Evolution-inducing move introduced in Pokémon Legends: Arceus to lack an Evolution effect, as Stantler cannot evolve into Wyrdeer in the games.

Title: Power Shift (move)

Power Shift (Japanese: パワーシフト Power Shift) is a non-damaging Normal-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. Power Shift switches the user's Attack and Special Attack stats with its Defense and Special Defense stats, respectively. Power Shift switches the user's raw Attack stat with its raw Defense stat. Stat modifiers are not swapped. Although Power Shift can be used in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, no Pokémon that can know the move naturally are in those games, and Metronome does not select this move. This makes it the only move that is legally usable in only a singular game.

Title: Stone Axe (move)

Stone Axe (Japanese: がんせきアックス Stone Axe) is a damage-dealing Rock-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Kleavor. Stone Axe inflicts damage and has an increased critical-hit ratio. It also leaves the target with splinters for 4 turns (Strong Style), 2 turns (Agile Style), or 3 turns (otherwise). Stone Axe inflicts damage and sets up Stealth Rock on the opposing side of the field. If used by a Pokémon with Sharpness, its power is increased by 50%. It does not have an increased critical-hit ratio.

Title: Springtide Storm (move)

Springtide Storm (Japanese: はるのあらし Spring Storm) is a damage-dealing Fairy-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Enamorus. Springtide Storm inflicts damage. If used by Incarnate Forme Enamorus or a Pokémon that mimicked the move, it has a chance of boosting user's offensive and defensive stats. In its Therian Forme, it has a chance of lowering the target's offensive and defensive stats. Springtide Storm's base power was increased to 100. In a battle with multiple opponents, Springtide Storm will target all adjacent opponents. Enamorus's form no longer influences its additional effect; it always has a 30% chance of lowering each target's Attack by one stage. Unlike its variations Bleakwind Storm, Wildbolt Storm, and Sandsear Storm, Springtide Storm's accuracy is unaffected by rain, and its maximum PP did not change in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Title: Mystical Power (move)

Mystical Power (Japanese: しんぴのちから Mysterious Power) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of the lake guardians. Mystical Power inflicts damage. If the sum of the user's current Attack and Sp. Atk stats is greater than or equal to the sum of its Defense and Sp. Def stats, the user's offensive stats will be raised after the move hits. Otherwise, its defensive stats will be raised. Mystical Power inflicts damage and raises the user's Special Attack by one stage.

Title: Raging Fury (move)

Raging Fury (Japanese: だいふんげき Great Infuriation) is a damage-dealing Fire-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. Raging Fury inflicts damage and the user becomes fixated on using this move until the battle ends, or until another move is chosen. Raging Fury's power and accuracy were changed to 120 and 100% respectively. Raging Fury inflicts damage for 2-3 turns, then the user becomes confused due to fatigue. It is disrupted if it is not successful due to missing, sleeping, paralysis, freeze, flinching, a protected target, or immunity. If a disruption occurs on what would have been the final, confusion-inducing turn of Raging Fury, the user will still become confused.

Title: Wave Crash (move)

Wave Crash (Japanese: ウェーブタックル Wave Tackle) is a damage-dealing Water-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. Wave Crash inflicts damage and the user receives recoil damage equal to 33% of the damage done to the target and raises the user's action speed. Wave Crash is the only Water-type move that causes recoil damage. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Wave Crash has the lowest base power of all recoil moves (besides Struggle).

Title: Chloroblast (move)

Chloroblast (Japanese: クロロブラスト Chloroblast) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Hisuian Electrode. Chloroblast inflicts damage, and the user takes damage equal to half of its maximum HP rounded up. It also lowers the user's action speed. Chloroblast has a base power of 150. The user takes recoil damage equal to half of its maximum HP rounded up unless the user has Magic Guard or Rock Head, or the target has Sap Sipper; its Speed is not lowered. This move's name may be a pun on chloroplast, a chlorophyll-rich organelle in plant and algae cells responsible for photosynthesis. Chloroblast is tied with Frenzy Plant for the highest base power of all Grass-type attacks, excluding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. Chloroblast is the only move to have its recoil negated by Rock Head but not receive a boost from Reckless.

Title: Mountain Gale (move)

Mountain Gale (Japanese: ひょうざんおろし Iceberg Drop) is a damage-dealing Ice-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Hisuian Avalugg. Mountain Gale inflicts damage and lowers the user's action speed. Mountain Gale's PP was changed to 10 and has a 30% chance of causing the target to flinch. Mountain Gale’s Japanese name, ひょうざんおろし “Hyōzan Oroshi” (Iceberg Wind), has a double meaning, asthe word “oroshi” can refer both to a wind blowing down a mountain (颪) and to the act of dropping something (下ろし). The first meaning is supported by the translations of the move in other languages, which consistently associate it with wind, while the second meaning is supported by the move’s animation, which shows chunks of ice being dropped onto the foe.

Title: Victory Dance (move)

Victory Dance (Japanese: しょうりのまい Victory Dance) is a non-damaging Fighting-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Hisuian Lilligant. It can be thought of as a physical-oriented counterpart to Quiver Dance. Victory Dance raises the user's offensive and defensive stats, in addition to giving a 50% boost to the damage dealt by the user's moves. Victory Dance raises the user's Attack, Defense, and Speed stats by one stage each.

Title: Headlong Rush (move)

Headlong Rush (Japanese: ぶちかまし Headlong Rush) is a damage-dealing Ground-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. Headlong Rush inflicts damage and then lowers the user's defensive stats for three turns. Headlong Rush inflicts damage and then lowers the user's Defense stat and Special Defense stat by one stage each. Headlong Rush is tied with Precipice Blades for the highest base power of all Ground-type moves, excluding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. In Japanese, ぶちかまし Buchikamashi (Headlong Rush) is a sumo term which refers to the frontal collision of the two sumo wrestlers right at the beginning of a match. The inspiration for the move's Ground type is likely a play with the words 武 bu (bravery, courage), ち chi (ground), ちか chika (underground) and 噛まし kamashi (blow, hit). This is also the likely reason why this move is considered a punching move, and why it can be learned by Hariyama. In Super Smash Bros. Ultimate, Charizard's forward smash is also known as Headlong Rush, though Charizard cannot learn the move in the core series games. This game was released prior to Headlong Rush's introduction as a move in the core series.

Title: Barb Barrage (move)

Barb Barrage (Japanese: どくばりセンボン Thousand Stingers) is a damage-dealing Poison-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Qwilfish, Hisuian Qwilfish, and Overqwil. Barb Barrage deals damage and has a 30% chance to poison the target. Its power is doubled if the target is affected by a non-volatile status condition. Barb Barrage's PP was decreased to 10. It has a 50% chance of poisoning the target, and its power is doubled if the target is already poisoned. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, a Hisuian Qwilfish that has used Barb Barrage in the strong style at least 20 times can evolve into Overqwil. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, a Hisuian Qwilfish will evolve into Overqwil simply by leveling up while knowing this move. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, a Hisuan Qwilfish will evolve into Overqwil by having Barb Barrage hit 20 targets.

Title: Esper Wing (move)

Esper Wing (Japanese: オーラウイング Aura Wing) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Hisuian Braviary. Esper Wing inflicts damage and has an increased critical hit ratio. It also raises the user's action speed. Esper Wing now has a base power of 80 and an accuracy of 100%. It has an increased critical hit ratio and raises the user's Speed by one stage. Description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021): Has a very small chance of leaving the target confused.

Title: Bitter Malice (move)

Bitter Malice (Japanese: うらみつらみ Bitter Grudge) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Hisuian Zorua and Zoroark. Bitter Malice inflicts damage and has a 30% chance (50% chance in the strong style) to leave the target with frostbite. The move's power is doubled if the target has a status condition. Bitter Malice's PP and power were changed to 10 and 75, respectively. It no longer powers up if the target has a status condition, and it instead lowers the target's Attack by one stage. Bitter Malice's Japanese name contains a possible pun, as Urami Tsurami is a rhyme.

Title: Shelter (move)

Shelter (Japanese: たてこもる Barricade) is a non-damaging Steel-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Hisuian Sliggoo and Goodra, and in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Goomy as well. Shelter raises the user's defensive stats in addition to making opponents' moves less likely to hit the user.

Title: Triple Arrows (move)

Triple Arrows (Japanese: ３ぼんのや Triple Arrows) is a damage-dealing Fighting-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Hisuian Decidueye. Triple Arrows inflicts damage. For three turns, it increases the user's critical hit ratio by two stages and decreases the target's defensive stats. Triple Arrows's PP was decreased to 10, its power was increased to 90, and it now has an increased critical hit ratio. It has a 50% chance of lowering the target's Defense by one stage and a 30% chance of causing the target to flinch.

Title: Infernal Parade (move)

Infernal Parade (Japanese: ひゃっきやこう Infernal Parade) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Hisuian Typhlosion. Infernal Parade inflicts damage and has a 30% chance of leaving a burn. The move's power is doubled if the target has a status condition. Infernal Parade is the only Ghost-type move that can cause burns. The move's Japanese name, ひゃっきやこう Hyakki Yakō, is a reference to the idiom 百鬼夜行 hyakki yakō (literally "Hundred demons traveling at night"), a term from Japanese folklore similar to the concept of pandemonium in English.

Title: Ceaseless Edge (move)

Ceaseless Edge (Japanese: ひけん・ちえなみ Secret Sword - Thousand Waves) is a damage-dealing Dark-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Hisuian Samurott. Ceaseless Edge inflicts damage and has an increased critical-hit ratio. It also leaves the target with splinters for 4 turns (Strong Style), 2 turns (Agile Style), or 3 turns (otherwise). Ceaseless Edge inflicts damage and sets up one layer of Spikes on the opposing side of the field. If used by a Pokémon with Sharpness, its power is increased by 50%. It does not have an increased critical-hit ratio.

Title: Bleakwind Storm (move)

Bleakwind Storm (Japanese: こがらしあらし Wintry Wind Storm) is a damage-dealing Flying-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Tornadus. Bleakwind Storm inflicts damage and has a 30% chance (50% chance in the strong style) to leave the target with frostbite. Bleakwind Storm's PP and power were changed to 10 and 100, respectively. It now has a 30% chance of lowering each target's Speed by one stage. In a battle with multiple opponents, Bleakwind Storm will target all adjacent opponents. When used during rain, Bleakwind Storm bypasses accuracy checks to always hit, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly. In Pokémon GO, Bleakwind Storm is a Charged Attack that has been available since January 10, 2024.

Title: Wildbolt Storm (move)

Wildbolt Storm (Japanese: かみなりあらし Thunderous Storm) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Thundurus. Wildbolt Storm inflicts damage and has a 30% chance of paralyzing the target. Wildbolt Storm's PP and power were changed to 10 and 100, respectively. It now has a 20% chance of paralyzing each target. In a battle with multiple opponents, Wildbolt Storm will target all adjacent opponents. When used during rain, Wildbolt Storm bypasses accuracy checks to always hit, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly. In Pokémon GO, Wildbolt Storm is a Charged Attack that has been available since January 17, 2024.

Title: Sandsear Storm (move)

Sandsear Storm (Japanese: ねっさのあらし Hot-Sand Storm) is a damage-dealing Ground-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Landorus. Sandsear Storm inflicts damage and has a 30% chance of burning the target. Sandsear Storm's PP was raised to 10 and its base power was increased to 100. It now has a 20% chance to burn each target. In battles with multiple opponents, Sandsear Storm targets all adjacent opponents. When used during rain, Sandsear Storm bypasses accuracy checks to always hit, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly. In Pokémon GO, Sandsear Storm is a Charged Attack that has been available since January 24, 2024. Sandsear Storm's description in Pokémon Legends: Arceus states that it "leaves the target with a burn", which erroneously implies that its additional effect is guaranteed. This error is not present in its variations' description.

Title: Lunar Blessing (move)

Lunar Blessing (Japanese: みかづきのいのり Crescent Prayer) is a non-damaging Psychic-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is one of the signature moves of Cresselia. Lunar Blessing heals the user of any status conditions and restores a variable quantity of the user's maximum HP. It also obscures the user, making attacks more likely to miss. Lunar Blessing's PP was reduced to 5. Lunar Blessing restores the HP of the user and its allies by 25% of their maximum HP, and heals any non-volatile status conditions they may have.

Title: Take Heart (move)

Take Heart (Japanese: ブレイブチャージ Brave Charge) is a non-damaging Psychic-type move introduced during Generation VIII, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is the signature move of Phione and one of the signature moves of Manaphy. Take Heart cures the user of all status conditions and raises its offensive and defensive stats for four turns. Take Heart raises the user's Special Attack and Special Defense stats by one stage each, and heals any non-volatile status conditions the user may have. Take Heart is the last Generation VIII move in index number order.

Title: Tera Blast (move)

Tera Blast (Japanese: テラバースト Tera Burst) is a damage-dealing Normal-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is TM171 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Tera Blast deals damage. If the user's Attack stat is higher than its Special Attack stat, Tera Blast becomes a physical move when Terastallized; otherwise, it remains a special move. For determining which stat is higher, stat stage-modifiers are taken into account, but other effects (e.g. held items such as a Choice Band, Abilities such as Huge Power, etc.) are not. This is determined when the move is performed (not when it is selected), so for example the move's category may change mid-turn if the opponent goes first and uses a move like Charm. If the user has Terastallized, Tera Blast's type becomes the same as their Tera Type, is unaffected by moves and Abilities that change its type, and has a different animation depending on the move's type (except if their Tera Type is Normal, where the animation stays the same whether Terastallized or not). Gale Wings does not increase the priority of Flying-type Tera Blast. If the user has Terastallized into the Stellar type, it deals supereffective damage against Terastallized targets and neutral damage against all other targets. Moreover, its base power is 100 instead of 80 and it lowers the user’s Attack and Sp. Atk stats by one stage. If the user is not Terastallized, Tera Blast will be affected by Abilities such as Galvanize, but if the user Terastallized into a Normal type, Tera Blast will remain Normal. Rainbow Jewel Tera Blast, Ancient Indigo Disk Tera Blast: Despite being made of Terastal energy and being responsible for the Terastal phenomenon, Terapagos cannot learn Tera Blast. It can, however, use the move in Pokémon Masters EX, creating a game move error. Tera Blast is the first Generation IX move in index number order. Tera Blast is the only move to have a different description depending on its type. Tera Blast hints at Glimmet and Glimmora's implied connection to the Terastal phenomenon, as eight Glimmet Crystals are needed to make the TM.

Title: Silk Trap (move)

Silk Trap (Japanese: スレッドトラップ Thread Trap) is a non-damaging Bug-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Spidops. Silk Trap protects the user from all effects of physical and special moves that target it during the turn it is used. If Silk Trap blocks an attack that would make contact with the user, the attacker's Speed stat is lowered by one stage. (Moves that are blocked do not activate other effects that require making contact, like Rocky Helmet or Rough Skin.) If the user goes last in the turn, the move will fail. The chance that Silk Trap will succeed also drops each time the user successfully and consecutively uses Endure, any protection move that only affects the user, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard. Each time, the chance of success is divided by 3. Some moves will do damage through Silk Trap. Moves that break protection, like Feint, will do their full damage and remove the effects of Silk Trap. The Ability Unseen Fist allows contact moves to bypass Silk Trap. Silk Trap is an increased priority move that will go before most attacks in a turn.

Title: Axe Kick (move)

Axe Kick (Japanese: かかとおとし Axe Kick) is a damaging-dealing Fighting-type move introduced in Generation IX. Axe Kick inflicts damage and has a 30% chance of confusing the target. If it misses, the user takes crash damage equal to half of its maximum HP. The user will also take crash damage if this move is used on a protected target, or if the target is immune to this move. This is the first crash damage move introduced since Generation I.

Title: Last Respects (move)

Last Respects (Japanese: おはかまいり Visit to the Grave) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type move introduced in Generation IX. Last Respects inflicts damage. Its base power, which starts at 50, is increased by an additional 50 each time a Pokémon on the user's side faints during a battle. Revived Pokémon who then faint again also count, with the base power capping at 5,050 after Pokémon faint 100 times in the same battle. Last Respects can reach the highest base power of all moves, capping at 5,050. It is the only move whose base power can be more than 3 digits long.

Title: Lumina Crash (move)

Lumina Crash (Japanese: ルミナコリジョン Lumina Collision) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Espathra. Lumina Crash inflicts damage and lowers the target's Special Defense by two stages.

Title: Order Up (move)

Order Up (Japanese: いっちょうあがり Order Up) is a damage-dealing Dragon-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Dondozo. Order Up inflicts damage. If the user has a Tatsugiri in its mouth via the Commander Ability, the move's animation will change and the user will gain a stat boost based on Tatsugiri's form: Order Up is boosted by Sheer Force even if Commander is not activated, and increases to stats (if any) apply normally. Prior to Version 1.2.0 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Order Up would increase the stats of a Dondozo with a Tatsugiri even if the move was negated (eg. via Protect). This was due to a bug and was fixed in the Version 1.2.0 update.

Title: Jet Punch (move)

Jet Punch (Japanese: ジェットパンチ Jet Punch) is a damage-dealing Water-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Palafin. Jet Punch inflicts damage. It has a priority of +1, so is used before all moves that do not have increased priority. Due to an oversight, Jet Punch used to have its damage boosted by Sheer Force despite not having any additional effects. This was later patched.

Title: Spicy Extract (move)

Spicy Extract (Japanese: ハバネロエキス Habanero Extract) is a non-damaging Grass-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Scovillain. Spicy Extract raises the target's Attack and lowers the target's Defense, each by two stages. It bypasses accuracy checks to always hit, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly.

Title: Spin Out (move)

Spin Out (Japanese: ホイールスピン Wheel Spin) is a damage-dealing Steel-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Varoom and Revavroom. Spin Out inflicts damage and lowers the user's Speed by two stages. Spin Out has a unique animation if it is used by a Starmobile. It shares this trait with Steel Roller.

Title: Population Bomb (move)

Population Bomb (Japanese: ネズミざん Proliferation) is a damage-dealing Normal-type multistrike move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Tandemaus and Maushold. Population Bomb inflicts damage, hitting the target up to ten times per use. Each of Population Bomb's strikes has a separate accuracy check. If any strike fails, the move ends. If the user is holding Loaded Dice, only one accuracy check is performed, and it will hit between four and ten times (with an even chance of each number of hits). If the user has Skill Link, the move will have only one accuracy check and hit the maximum ten times. If used by a Pokémon with Sharpness, its power is increased by 50%. If the target has an Ability that activates upon contact, each strike counts individually, enabling the Ability to activate multiple times. If the target is holding a Rocky Helmet, the user is damaged for each strike. If a Focus Sash activates before the last strike is dealt, the following strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can still activate repeatedly to prevent fainting, but each chance is independent. Sturdy will activate with each hit if the defending Pokémon has a maximum HP of 1. Assuming no modifications to accuracy or evasion, this move's average effective power is ~117.24, with an average number of hits of ~6, and the chances of it hitting at least a certain number of times are as follows: Along with Triple Kick and Triple Axel, Population Bomb is one of only three multistrike moves whose chances to miss for successive hits are independent of the first hit. The Japanese name for the move can be read as both ねずみ算 nezumizan (to multiply like mice; literally "mouse mathematics", a common arithmetic problem) and ねずみ斬 nezumizan (mouse cut), explaining why the move is strengthened by Sharpness. The English name for the move could be inspired by the 1968 book The Population Bomb by W, which incorrectly predicted worldwide famine and death due to overpopulation that would arise in the 1970s.

Title: Ice Spinner (move)

Ice Spinner (Japanese: アイススピナー Ice Spinner) is a damage-dealing Ice-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is TM124 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Ice Spinner inflicts damage and removes any terrain present on the battlefield. However, if the user faints due to its held Life Orb, the target's held Rocky Helmet, or the target having the Ability Rough Skin or Iron Barbs, or the user is forced to switch out due to the target's Red Card, the terrain is not removed. The evolutionary lines of Seel, Shellder, Pineco, Dunsparce, Bronzor, Popplio, and Mareanie, plus Glalie, Froslass, Cryogonal, Hisuian Sliggoo, and Frosmoth, can all learn this move — in which the user "covers its feet in thin ice" — despite not having feet.

Title: Glaive Rush (move)

Glaive Rush (Japanese: きょけんとつげき Giant Sword Assault) is a damage-dealing Dragon-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Baxcalibur. Glaive Rush inflicts damage. If the move hits, until the next time the user acts, any attacks directed at the user (including OHKO moves) deal double the damage (not applicable to moves that deal direct damage) and bypass accuracy and evasion checks to always hit. The effect ends if a different move is used. This move is likely a reference to the Godzilla film Godzilla vs. Hedorah, which features Godzilla propelling himself in a similar using his atomic breath, while the scene was invented as a presumed reference to Gamera by the director Yoshimitsu Banno who later launched a scrapped Gamera project, which was subsequently redeveloped into the 2014 film Godzilla.

Title: Revival Blessing (move)

Revival Blessing (Japanese: さいきのいのり Recovery Prayer) is a non-damaging Normal-type move introduced in Generation IX. Revival Blessing allows the player to select a fainted Pokémon in the party; the selected Pokémon will be revived, and half of its maximum HP will be restored. Revival Blessing will fail if there are no fainted Pokémon in the party. This move cannot be copied by Sketch. Revival Blessing cannot be used if the user is under the effect of Heal Block caused by Psychic Noise. Revival Blessing is one of two moves (excluding Struggle) whose base PP is not a multiple of 5, the other being Sketch. Additionally, both Sketch and Revival Blessing cannot have their maximum PP increased. The only Pokémon that can learn this move, Pawmot and Rabsca, learn it upon Evolution, with the condition for their Evolution being leveling up while outside of their Poké Ball after walking 1,000 steps using the Let's Go! feature. Not even Smeargle can learn this move, as Sketch fails to copy it. Revival Blessing is the only game mechanic that can revive a fainted Pokémon during a battle without using items from the Bag. Consequently, it is the only game mechanic that can revive a Pokémon in battles against other players in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Title: Salt Cure (move)

Salt Cure (Japanese: しおづけ Salting) is a damage-dealing Rock-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Naclstack and Garganacl. Salt Cure inflicts 1/8 of the target's maximum HP as damage per turn in addition to the damage dealt when it is used. If a Steel and/or Water type is affected by Salt Cure, the amount of damage per turn is ¼ of its maximum HP. The effect is removed when the afflicted Pokémon is switched out. Salt Cure cannot be passed by Baton Pass. Team Star's Starmobiles are unaffected by the secondary effects of Salt Cure.

Title: Triple Dive (move)

Triple Dive (Japanese: トリプルダイブ Triple Dive) is a damage-dealing Water-type multistrike move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Wugtrio. Triple Dive inflicts damage, hitting the target three times per use. If a Focus Sash activates before the last strike is dealt, the following strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can still activate repeatedly to prevent fainting, but each chance is independent. Sturdy will activate with each hit if the defending Pokémon has a maximum HP of 1.

Title: Mortal Spin (move)

Mortal Spin (Japanese: キラースピン Killer Spin) is a damage-dealing Poison-type move introduced in Generation IX. Mortal Spin inflicts damage and then removes any binding effects and Leech Seed from the user, as well as entry hazards from the user's side of the field (unless the user has fainted as a result of the move, such as from Rough Skin). It also leaves each target poisoned. In a battle with multiple opponents, it will target all adjacent opponents. In Over the Top!, Geeta's Glimmora was mentioned to know Mortal Spin. However, before it could use the move, it was interrupted by Liko's Floragato using Sucker Punch, forcing Geeta to command the use of Spiky Shield instead. Mortal Spin's Japanese name, キラースピン Kirā Supin, may be a pun on both キラキラ kirakira (onomatopoeia for glittering) and キラー kirā (killer), like with Glimmora's Japanese name. In French, Italian, and Chinese, the "glittering" meaning is kept instead of the "killer" meaning.

Title: Doodle (move)

Doodle (Japanese: うつしえ Trace) is a non-damaging Normal-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Grafaiai. Doodle changes the Abilities of the user and any ally to the targeted Pokémon's Ability. Doodle will fail if either the target, the user, or the user's ally have any of the following Abilities: As One, Battle Bond, Comatose, Commander, Disguise, Gulp Missile, Ice Face, Multitype, Poison Puppeteer, Power Construct, Protosynthesis, Quark Drive, RKS System, Schooling, Shields Down, Stance Change, Zen Mode, or Zero to Hero. It will also fail if the target has Receiver, but not if the user or its ally has it.

Title: Fillet Away (move)

Fillet Away (Japanese: みをけずる Cut to the Bone) is a non-damaging Normal-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Veluza. Fillet Away deducts half of the user's maximum HP (rounded down) from its current HP and, in return, it raises the user's Attack, Special Attack, and Speed stats by two stages each. Fillet Away fails if the user's current HP is less than or equal to half its maximum, or if the user's aforementioned stats are all at +6 stages. The move's Japanese name, みをけずる (Mi o Kezuru), which can be translated literally as 'to chip away at one's body,' has the figurative meaning of 'enduring severe hardships,' generally in order to achieve a goal. Similarly, the move's European Spanish name, Deslome, can be understood as both 'cutting fish fillets' or 'wearing one's back out', normally from working too hard.

Title: Kowtow Cleave (move)

Kowtow Cleave (Japanese: ドゲザン Kowtow Slash) is a damage-dealing Dark-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Kingambit. Kowtow Cleave inflicts damage and bypasses accuracy checks to always hit, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly. If used by a Pokémon with Sharpness, its power is increased by 50%. Kowtow Cleave is based on dogeza, which is kneeling to a person of higher status. The word "kowtow" more accurately refers to a Chinese act that is similar to dogeza.

Title: Flower Trick (move)

Flower Trick (Japanese: トリックフラワー Trick Flower) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Meowscarada. Flower Trick inflicts damage and bypasses accuracy checks to always hit, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly. It will always result in a critical hit unless the target has Battle Armor or Shell Armor. In Pokémon GO, Flower Trick is a Charged Attack that has been available since January 5, 2025.

Title: Torch Song (move)

Torch Song (Japanese: フレアソング Flare Song) is a damage-dealing Fire-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Skeledirge. Torch Song is a sound-based move. It inflicts damage and raises the user's Special Attack by one stage. Torch Song can hit Pokémon even if they are behind a substitute. In Pokémon GO, Torch Song is a Charged Attack that has been available since March 8, 2025. Torch Song is the only Fire-type sound-based move.

Title: Aqua Step (move)

Aqua Step (Japanese: アクアステップ Aqua Step) is a damage-dealing Water-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Quaquaval. Aqua Step inflicts damage and raises the user's Speed by one stage if the move is successful. Clavell & Quaquaval's passive skill P-Move Stat Raises ×2 makes this move boost their Speed by two stat ranks after each use. In Pokémon GO, Aqua Step is a Charged Attack that has been available since July 20, 2025

Title: Raging Bull (move)

Raging Bull (Japanese: レイジングブル Raging Bull) is a damage-dealing Normal-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Tauros. Raging Bull removes Light Screen, Reflect, and Aurora Veil from the target's side of the field (even if it targets an ally), then inflicts damage. If the move fails to hit the target, the effects of the screens are not removed. If the user is Paldean Tauros, the move's type changes depending on its form. Combat Breed's Raging Bull is Fighting-type, Blaze Breed's Raging Bull is Fire-type, and Aqua Breed's Raging Bull is Water-type. If used by a Pokémon other than Paldean Tauros, the move is Normal-type.

Title: Make It Rain (move)

Make It Rain (Japanese: ゴールドラッシュ Gold Rush) is a damage-dealing Steel-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Gholdengo. Make It Rain inflicts damage and lowers the user's Special Attack by one stage. In a battle with multiple opponents, Make It Rain will target all adjacent opponents. For each target the move hits, coins are scattered on the ground. $5 multiplied by the user's level are scattered each time. No coins are scattered in Tera Raid Battles. If the player wins the battle, they will pick up all the scattered coins and receive the total money amount. The amount of money from Make It Rain is doubled if any of the player's Pokémon takes part in battle while holding an Amulet Coin or Luck Incense; however, this effect can only be applied once, regardless of how many additional Pokémon hold this item. Having more than one Pokémon holding the item does not increase the money amount any further. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, coins scattered by Make It Rain are picked up immediately. Make It Rain does not scatter coins when hitting objects like Mega Crystals. The amount of money from Make It Rain is unaffected by the Gold Canari Plush. Sygna Suit Lear & Gholdengo's passive skill Golden Authority prevents this move from lowering their Sp. Atk after use. "Make it rain" is an idiom in the English language which means to make wealth or to show off wealth; in the latter sense, it often refers to the act of throwing money into the air, to make it appear as if it is raining money. Due to this, despite its English name, this move does not actually summon rain. The move's rename in English from "Gold Rush" may be due to a naming conflict with G-Max Gold Rush, a G-Max Move introduced in Pokémon Sword and Shield.

Title: Psyblade (move)

Psyblade (Japanese: サイコブレイド Psycho Blade) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced during Generation IX. It is the signature move of Iron Leaves. Psyblade inflicts damage. If there is Electric Terrain on the field, its base power is increased to 120, whether or not the user is grounded. If used by a Pokémon with Sharpness, its power is increased by 50%. Among the signature moves of the six Paradox Pokémon connected to Legendary Pokémon, Psyblade is the only one to not match its user's primary type, namely Grass. Psyblade is the only non-Electric-type move to benefit from a power boost from Electric Terrain.

Title: Hydro Steam (move)

Hydro Steam (Japanese: ハイドロスチーム Hydro Steam) is a damage-dealing Water-type move introduced during Generation IX. It is the signature move of Walking Wake. Hydro Steam inflicts damage. If used under harsh sunlight, it receives a 50% damage boost, unlike all other Water-type moves, which would receive a 50% damage reduction in harsh sunlight instead. If used by a Pokémon holding a Utility Umbrella during harsh sunlight, it does not receive the 50% damage boost, instead being affected by the standard 50% damage reduction for Water-type moves. Hydro Steam is the only non-Fire-type move to benefit from a power boost from harsh sunlight.

Title: Ruination (move)

Ruination (Japanese: カタストロフィ Catastrophe) is a damage-dealing Dark-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of the treasures of ruin. Ruination deals damage equal to 50% of the opponent's remaining HP, rounded down. It always deals at least 1 HP of damage.

Title: Collision Course (move)

Collision Course (Japanese: アクセルブレイク Accel Break) is a damage-dealing Fighting-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Koraidon. Collision Course inflicts damage. If the move is super effective on the target, its damage is increased by roughly 33% (or more precisely, by a factor of 5461/4096).

Title: Electro Drift (move)

Electro Drift (Japanese: イナズマドライブ Lightning Drive) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Miraidon. Electro Drift inflicts damage. If the move is super effective on the target, its damage is increased by roughly 33% (or more precisely, by a factor of 5461/4096).

Title: Shed Tail (move)

Shed Tail (Japanese: しっぽきり Tail Shedding) is a non-damaging Normal-type move introduced in Generation IX. Shed Tail creates a substitute at the cost of half of the user's maximum HP (rounded up), then switches the user out. The created substitute has 1/4 of the user's maximum HP. The Pokémon that switches in will then have the substitute. If the user is holding a Berry that would trigger in response to Shed Tail (such as a Sitrus Berry), it eats its held Berry before switching out. Shed Tail fails (and will not switch the user out) if the user already has a substitute, if the user has no teammates to switch to, or if the user's HP is less than or equal to 50% of its maximum HP. The substitute will not protect the sent out Pokémon against entry hazards. The Japanese name of this move, しっぽきり Shippokiri, comes from the idiomatic expression 蜥蜴の尻尾切り tokage no shippo kiri (lit. cutting lizard's tail), which means to shift one's work or responsibility onto a subordinate.

Title: Chilly Reception (move)

Chilly Reception (Japanese: さむいギャグ Cold Gag) is a non-damaging Ice-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Slowking and one of the signature moves of Galarian Slowking. Chilly Reception creates snow on the battlefield and switches the user out. Snow instigated by this move lasts five turns (or eight turns if the user is holding an Icy Rock). The user will still switch out even if there is already snow on the field when the move is used. If there are no other teammates to switch to, it will only change the weather to snow. If the move is selected directly, before using the move, the following message is displayed: "<Pokémon> is preparing to tell a chillingly bad joke!" even if the move fails to execute. Using this move through a move that calls another move will not display the aforementioned message. In Japanese, Korean, and Chinese, a "cold" joke is often used to refer to a joke that has no punchline, or one that fails to get a laugh from its audience. An unamused audience may remark that the room is suddenly cold. A similar joke in the English language is to pretend to only hear crickets respond to a bad joke. An exaggeration of this response in media, particularly in comedies, is to have the room literally become cold as a result of a bad joke. This is reflected in-game by the user creating a snowstorm, then leaving the battlefield, presumably ashamed of its terrible joke.

Title: Tidy Up (move)

Tidy Up (Japanese: おかたづけ Tidy Up) is a non-damaging Normal-type move introduced in Generation IX. Tidy Up raises the user's Attack stat and Speed stat by one stage each. It also clears away substitutes, Spikes, Toxic Spikes, Stealth Rock, and Sticky Web on both the user's and opponent's side of the field. The user's stats will still be raised even if there are no traps or substitutes to remove.

Title: Snowscape (move)

Snowscape (Japanese: ゆきげしき Snowscape) is a non-damaging Ice-type move introduced in Generation IX, replacing Hail from earlier generations. It is TM052 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. When Snowscape is used, a snowstorm will begin on the field. This effect will last for 5 turns. This clears any other type of weather, but will fail if there is already a snowstorm. However, it cannot override weather that occurs in the overworld. All Ice-type Pokémon have their Defense raised by 50% for the duration of the snowstorm. Pokémon with the Ice Body Ability recover 1⁄16 of their maximum HP each round. Pokémon with the Snow Cloak Ability increase their evasiveness by 20%. Pokémon with the Slush Rush Ability have their Speed doubled. If an Icy Rock is held by the Pokémon using Snowscape at the time of use, the duration of Snowscape will increase from 5 to 8 turns. The snowstorm allows Aurora Veil to be used. While Snowscape is in effect, Synthesis, Morning Sun, and Moonlight will recover only ¼ of the user's maximum HP. Additionally, the move Weather Ball becomes Ice-type.

Title: Pounce (move)

Pounce (Japanese: とびつく Pounce) is a damage-dealing Bug-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is TM021 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Pounce inflicts damage and lowers the target's Speed by one stage.

Title: Trailblaze (move)

Trailblaze (Japanese: くさわけ Trailblazer) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is TM020 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet and TM115 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Trailblaze inflicts damage and raises the user's Speed by one stage.

Title: Chilling Water (move)

Chilling Water (Japanese: ひやみず Cold Water) is a damage-dealing Water-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is TM022 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet and TM110 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Chilling Water inflicts damage and lowers the target's Attack by one stage. In Pokémon GO, Chilling Water is a Charged Attack that has been available since September 14, 2025.

Title: Hyper Drill (move)

Hyper Drill (Japanese: ハイパードリル Hyper Drill) is a damage-dealing Normal-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Dunsparce and Dudunsparce. Hyper Drill inflicts damage. It bypasses the effects of Protect, Detect, Spiky Shield, King's Shield, Baneful Bunker, Silk Trap, and Burning Bulwark, but does not lift the effects of these moves. A Dunsparce that knows this move will evolve into Dudunsparce when leveled up.

Title: Twin Beam (move)

Twin Beam (Japanese: ツインビーム Twin Beam) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type multistrike move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Girafarig and Farigiraf. Twin Beam inflicts damage, hitting twice per use. Each strike has an equal chance to be a critical hit. Twin Beam will hit again if the first strike breaks a substitute. Mirror Coat will only acknowledge the second strike of this move. If a Focus Sash activates before the last strike is dealt, the following strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can still activate repeatedly to prevent fainting, but each chance is independent. Sturdy will activate with each hit if the defending Pokémon is a Shedinja. If the user is holding a King's Rock or Razor Fang, each consecutive hit has an equal chance to cause the opponent to flinch. If the target has Stamina, each strike will activate it. A Girafarig that knows Twin Beam evolves into Farigiraf when leveled up. Twin Beam is the only Psychic-type multistrike move.

Title: Rage Fist (move)

Rage Fist (Japanese: ふんどのこぶし Rage Fist) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Primeape and Annihilape. Rage Fist inflicts damage. Its power increases by 50 each time the user is hit during the battle by a move that causes direct damage, up to a maximum of 350 (after 6 hits). This includes moves used by either allies or opponents and includes moves that do not actually deplete HP, such as those that activate a Disguise. However, the power of Rage Fist does not increase if the user attacks itself due to confusion, nor does it increase when the user's substitute is struck. Each strike from a multistrike move counts as one hit. Switching out or fainting does not reset the hit counter. Transforming copies the target's hit counter. A Primeape that has used Rage Fist (and had PP deducted with each use whenever or not the move dealt damage) at least 20 times evolves into Annihilape when leveled up, or, in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, gains the option to evolve. Extra PP lost through effects such as Pressure does not count as extra uses. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, Rage Fist does not have to hit Pokémon to evolve Primeape. In Pokémon GO, Rage Fist is a Charged Attack that has been available since November 10, 2024.

Title: Armor Cannon (move)

Armor Cannon (Japanese: アーマーキャノン Armor Cannon) is a damage-dealing Fire-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Armarouge. Armor Cannon deals damage and lowers the user's Defense and Special Defense stats by one stage each. Armor Cannon is the only variation of Superpower to be a special move.

Title: Bitter Blade (move)

Bitter Blade (Japanese: むねんのつるぎ Mortification Sword) is a damage-dealing Fire-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Ceruledge. Bitter Blade inflicts damage, and it restores the user's HP by half of the damage dealt. If used by a Pokémon with Sharpness, its power is increased by 50%. If the user is holding a Big Root, the HP restored is increased by 30%. Big Root does not increase the damage dealt. If the target has the Ability Liquid Ooze, the user will lose the amount of HP it would have gained instead. Bitter Blade is the only Fire-type HP-draining move. Bitter Blade was the first Generation IX move seen in the animated series. As shown in Riding the Waves, if Bitter Blade is used to cut food it will suck out its nutrients but the food will still be edible.

Title: Double Shock (move)

Double Shock (Japanese: でんこうそうげき Double Lightning Attack) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Pawmot. If used by an Electric-type Pokémon, Double Shock will inflict damage to the target and cause the user to lose its Electric type. Double Shock fails if the user is not Electric-type, making the move always fail after the first use until the Pokémon regains its Electric type (either by switching out, or other type changing effects such as through Protean or Reflect Type). If the user is a pure Electric-type Pokémon, it will become typeless. The Pokémon's type reverts to its default types if it is switched out. A Pokémon with Protean or Libero cannot successfully use Double Shock unless it already is Electric-type; otherwise, Double Shock will fail and Protean or Libero will not activate. A Pokémon that has Terastallized into its Electric Tera Type will not lose its Electric type (although the message "The <wild/opposing> <Pokémon> used up all its electricity!" is still displayed). Therefore, a Terastallized Pokémon can use Double Shock multiple times in a row without the need to switch out. This buddy move was known as B Double Shock prior to version 2.38.0. If the player views the summary of a Pokémon that is currently typeless, such as through the use of Double Shock, the game may crash.

Title: Gigaton Hammer (move)

Gigaton Hammer (Japanese: デカハンマー Huge Hammer) is a damage-dealing Steel-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of Tinkaton. Gigaton Hammer inflicts damage. The user cannot select Gigaton Hammer in succession unless the move failed. If the player attempts to use Gigaton Hammer twice in a row, they will be prevented from doing so, and will be allowed to select another move. Once another move is used, Gigaton Hammer can be used again. If the user cannot select another move (due to Choice items or having no other usable moves), it will use Struggle every other turn. Instruct and Sleep Talk allow Gigaton Hammer to be used twice in a row. Encore allows Gigaton Hammer to be used twice in a row, but on subsequent turns of the Encore the user will be forced to Struggle. Gigaton Hammer has the highest base power of all Steel-type moves, excluding Z-Moves. Gigaton Hammer has a different animation when used by a Pokémon other than Tinkaton. In this case, its animation is similar to those of Ice Hammer and Dragon Hammer, but with a silver hammer instead of blue or purple, respectively.

Title: Comeuppance (move)

Comeuppance (Japanese: ほうふく Retaliation) is a damage-dealing Dark-type move introduced in Generation IX. Comeuppance returns 1.5 times the damage dealt by the foe's last attack. Unlike Counter or Mirror Coat, Comeuppance does not require specifically physical or special damage sources and Comeuppance does not have decreased priority; if the user acts before it is hit by an opponent's damaging move, Comeuppance will fail. The move also fails if the user's substitute is hit instead. In battles involving multiple Pokémon, Comeuppance will hit the last opponent that dealt damage to the user. Comeuppance cannot affect ally Pokémon. However, if the last Pokémon to hit the user faints before the user moves, the remaining enemy Pokémon will be hit instead, like with a regular single-target damaging move (using the damage taken from the move of the now-fainted Pokémon that had hit the user). Comeuppance has the same description text as Metal Burst.

Title: Aqua Cutter (move)

Aqua Cutter (Japanese: アクアカッター Aqua Cutter) is a damage-dealing Water-type move introduced in Generation IX. Aqua Cutter inflicts damage and has an increased critical hit ratio. If used by a Pokémon with Sharpness, its power is increased by 50%. Aqua Cutter is the only physical Water-type move that does not make contact, outside of Z-Moves and Max Moves.

Title: Blazing Torque (move)

Blazing Torque (Japanese: バーンアクセル Burn Accel) is a damage-dealing Fire-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of the Schedar Starmobile. Blazing Torque inflicts damage and has a 30% chance of burning the target. Encore, Instruct, Mimic, and Sketch fail if the target's last used move was Blazing Torque. Copycat fails if the last used move was Blazing Torque. Only the Revavroom in the Schedar Squad's Starmobile can use this move.

Title: Wicked Torque (move)

Wicked Torque (Japanese: ダークアクセル Dark Accel) is a damage-dealing Dark-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of the Segin Starmobile. Wicked Torque inflicts damage and has a 10% chance of putting the target to sleep. Encore, Instruct, Mimic, and Sketch fail if the target's last used move was Wicked Torque. Copycat fails if the last used move was Wicked Torque. Only the Revavroom in the Segin Squad's Starmobile can use this move.

Title: Noxious Torque (move)

Noxious Torque (Japanese: ポイズンアクセル Poison Accel) is a damage-dealing Poison-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of the Navi Starmobile. Noxious Torque inflicts damage and has a 30% chance of poisoning the target. Encore, Instruct, Mimic, and Sketch fail if the target's last used move was Noxious Torque. Copycat fails if the last used move was Noxious Torque. Only the Revavroom in the Navi Squad's Starmobile can use this move.

Title: Combat Torque (move)

Combat Torque (Japanese: ファイトアクセル Fight Accel) is a damage-dealing Fighting-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of the Caph Starmobile. Combat Torque inflicts damage and has a 30% chance of paralyzing the target. Encore, Instruct, Mimic, and Sketch fail if the target's last used move was Combat Torque. Copycat fails if the last used move was Combat Torque. Only the Revavroom in the Caph Squad's Starmobile can use this move.

Title: Magical Torque (move)

Magical Torque (Japanese: マジカルアクセル Magical Accel) is a damage-dealing Fairy-type move introduced in Generation IX. It is the signature move of the Ruchbah Starmobile. Magical Torque inflicts damage and has a 30% chance of confusing the target. Encore, Instruct, Mimic, and Sketch fail if the target's last used move was Magical Torque.
