Copycat fails if the last used move was Magical Torque. Only the Revavroom in the Ruchbah Squad's Starmobile can use this move. Prior to the release of The Hidden Treasure of Area Zero expansion for Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Magical Torque was the only Fairy-type move to have been introduced in Generation IX. Magical Torque is the only physical Fairy-type move that does not make contact, outside of Z-Moves and Max Moves, though it is not accessible in regular gameplay.

Title: Blood Moon (move)

Blood Moon (Japanese: ブラッドムーン Blood Moon) is a damage-dealing Normal-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Teal Mask. It is the signature move of Bloodmoon Ursaluna. Blood Moon inflicts damage. The user cannot select Blood Moon in succession unless the move failed. If the player attempts to use Blood Moon twice in a row, they will be prevented from doing so, and will be allowed to select another move. Once another move is used, Blood Moon can be used again. If the user cannot select another move (due to Choice items or having no other usable moves), it will use Struggle every other turn. Instruct and Sleep Talk allow Blood Moon to be used twice in a row. Encore allows Blood Moon to be used twice in a row, but on subsequent turns of the Encore, the user will be forced to Struggle.

Title: Matcha Gotcha (move)

Matcha Gotcha (Japanese: シャカシャカほう Rustle-Rustle Cannon) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Teal Mask. It is the signature move of Sinistcha. Matcha Gotcha deals damage and restores the user's HP equal to up to half of the damage dealt. It also has a 20% chance of burning targets. Matcha Gotcha hits both opponents in a Double Battle. If the target has the Ability Liquid Ooze, the user will lose the amount of HP it would have gained instead. If Matcha Gotcha is used in a Double Battle when one of the opponents has the ability Liquid Ooze, the user will take damage from that ability first, and then heal of HP from the other opponent. If the user is holding a Big Root, the HP restored is increased by 30% (making the restored HP 65% of the damage dealt). Big Root does not increase the damage dealt. Upon execution, Matcha Gotcha will thaw the user if frozen. Matcha Gotcha also thaws a frozen target hit by it. HP-draining move that can target all adjacent opponents without affecting allies. Despite its Japanese name containing ほう "cannon", it is not one of the ball and bomb moves.

Title: Syrup Bomb (move)

Syrup Bomb (Japanese: みずあめボム Mizuame Bomb) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Teal Mask. It is the signature move of Dipplin and one of the signature moves of Hydrapple. Syrup Bomb inflicts damage and lowers the target's Speed stat by one stage each turn, for the next three turns. It does not affect targets with Bulletproof. If the move's user leaves the field, the effect will end immediately. The game uses a unique animation when the target's Speed stat is lowered. Syrup Bomb has a different appearance if the user is a Shiny Pokémon, a distinction shared with Mind Blown and Dragon Darts. Only Shiny Dipplin and Hydrapple will have both the initial attack and the subsequent additional effect use the yellow coloration. In other cases, the coloration differs depending on the user, as it appears to account for the current appearance, Shininess, and species of the user when determining what coloration to use: When used by other Pokémon, by means such as Metronome or Sketch, Syrup Bomb will always have the red coloration, regardless of whether the user is Shiny. When used by a Pokémon, regardless of whether it itself is Shiny or not, that has transformed into a Shiny Dipplin or Hydrapple, Syrup Bomb will correctly display the yellow coloration for the initial attack, but the additional effect that lowers Speed at the end of each turn will still use the non-Shiny red coloration.

Title: Ivy Cudgel (move)

Ivy Cudgel (Japanese: ツタこんぼう Ivy Cudgel) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Teal Mask. It is the signature move of Ogerpon. Ivy Cudgel deals damage and has an increased critical hit ratio. The move's type changes depending on Ogerpon's held mask: If used by a Pokémon other than Ogerpon, Ivy Cudgel will always be a Grass-type move, even if that Pokémon holds one of the masks.

Title: Electro Shot (move)

Electro Shot (Japanese: エレクトロビーム Electro Beam) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is the signature move of Archaludon. On the turn it is selected, Electro Shot will raise the user's Special Attack stat by one stage, and the message "[User] absorbed electricity!" will be shown. On the following turn, Electro Shot will do damage (factoring in the Special Attack boost), PP will be deducted from it, and it will count as the last move used. Once Electro Shot is selected, the user won't be able to switch out until it is disrupted or fully executed. Electro Shot will not need a turn to charge if used during rain. If Electro Shot charges up for a turn before it is used, it will not go off if Disable is used on it. Sleep, freezing, and flinching will pause but not disrupt the duration of Electro Shot. If a Power Herb is held by the user, Electro Shot can be executed in one turn. The user will charge and boost their Special Attack, consume the Power Herb, then execute the final stage of the move in the same turn. The Special Attack boost is applied because the charging stage is still performed. Electro Shot's damage is boosted by Sheer Force despite not suppressing any additional effects. Electro Shot is tied with Bolt Strike for having the highest base power of all Electric-type moves, excluding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves.

Title: Tera Starstorm (move)

Tera Starstorm (Japanese: テラクラスター Tera Cluster) is a damage-dealing move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is the signature move of Terapagos. Tera Starstorm inflicts damage. When used by Terapagos in its Stellar Form, Tera Starstorm becomes Stellar-type and targets both opponents in a Double Battle. If the user's Attack stat is higher than its Special Attack stat, Tera Starstorm becomes a physical move when Terastallized; otherwise, it remains a special move. For determining which stat is higher, stat stage-modifiers are taken into account, but other effects (e.g. held items such as a Choice Band, Abilities such as Huge Power, etc.) are not. Tera Starstorm has a different appearance if the user has Terastallized.

Title: Fickle Beam (move)

Fickle Beam (Japanese: きまぐレーザー Fickle Laser) is a damage-dealing Dragon-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is one of the signature moves of Hydrapple. Fickle Beam inflicts damage. It has a 30% chance to have its power doubled to 160. Consequently, the average base power is 104. Fickle Beam has a different appearance if the user "goes all out", where all of Hydrapple's syrpents shoot together in unison.

Title: Burning Bulwark (move)

Burning Bulwark (Japanese: かえんのまもり Blazing Protection) is a non-damaging Fire-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is the signature move of Gouging Fire. Burning Bulwark protects the user from all effects of physical and special moves that target it during the turn it is used. If Burning Bulwark blocks an attack that would make contact with the user, the attacker becomes burned (unless it is immune). (Moves that are blocked do not activate other effects that require making contact, like Rocky Helmet or Rough Skin.) If the user goes last in the turn, the move will fail. The chance that Burning Bulwark will succeed also drops each time the user successfully and consecutively uses Endure, any protection move that only affects the user, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard. Each time, the chance of success is divided by 3. Some moves will do damage through Burning Bulwark. Moves that break protection, like Feint, will do their full damage and remove the effects of Burning Bulwark. The Ability Unseen Fist allows contact moves to bypass Burning Bulwark. Burning Bulwark is an increased priority move that will go before most attacks in a turn. Burning Bulwark is the only protection move that is able to be called by Metronome. It is also the only signature move of a Paradox Pokémon to be a status move.

Title: Thunderclap (move)

If you were looking for the Thunderclap Pokémon, see Zeraora. Thunderclap (Japanese: じんらい Thunderclap) is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is one of the signature moves of Raging Bolt. Thunderclap has a priority of +1. If Thunderclap's target has selected a physical or special attack and has not yet had a chance to execute its move, Thunderclap will damage the target; otherwise, Thunderclap will fail. Thunderclap only depends on the move the target has selected, and is not affected by any consideration of whether the attack can actually be executed, such as status conditions like sleep or freeze or Abilities like Truant. This move's name is the same as the category of Zeraora.

Title: Mighty Cleave (move)

Mighty Cleave (Japanese: パワフルエッジ Powerful Edge) is a damage-dealing Rock-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is the signature move of Iron Boulder. Mighty Cleave inflicts damage. It bypasses the effects of Protect, Detect, Spiky Shield, King's Shield, Baneful Bunker, Silk Trap, and Burning Bulwark, but does not lift the effects of these moves. If used by a Pokémon with Sharpness, its power is increased by 50%.

Title: Tachyon Cutter (move)

Tachyon Cutter (Japanese: タキオンカッター Tachyon Cutter) is a damage-dealing Steel-type multistrike move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is the signature move of Iron Crown. Tachyon Cutter inflicts damage, hitting twice per use. Each strike has an equal chance to be a critical hit. It bypasses accuracy checks to always hit, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly. If used by a Pokémon with Sharpness, its power is increased by 50%. Tachyon Cutter will hit again if the first strike breaks a substitute. Mirror Coat will only acknowledge the second strike of this move. If a Focus Sash activates before the last strike is dealt, the following strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can still activate repeatedly to prevent fainting, but each chance is independent. If the user is holding a King's Rock or Razor Fang, each consecutive hit has an equal chance to cause the opponent to flinch. If the target has Stamina, each strike will activate it. Tachyon Cutter is the only multistrike move that cannot miss under normal conditions. A tachyon is a hypothetical particle that can move at superluminal (faster than light) speeds.

Title: Hard Press (move)

Hard Press (Japanese: ハードプレス Hard Press) is a damage-dealing Steel-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is TM225 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Hard Press inflicts damage. Its power varies between 1 and 100, and is greater the more HP the target has: This move was first revealed on November 21st, 2023, alongside Supercell Slam prior to the release of The Indigo Disk during Serebii's writeup of their hands-on preview of the DLC. This move works the same way as Crush Grip and Wring Out, but with higher PP and lower possible base power. It is the only one of these moves to not be Normal-type.

Title: Dragon Cheer (move)

Dragon Cheer (Japanese: ドラゴンエール Dragon Yell) is a non-damaging Dragon-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is TM226 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Dragon Cheer increases the user's allies' critical-hit ratio by one stage. If the affected Pokémon are Dragon-type, their critical-hit ratio is increased by two stages instead. The effects of Dragon Cheer can be copied by Psych Up, Transform, Costar, Opportunist, and a Mirror Herb. Dragon Cheer's effects apply based on the allies' type upon use of the move, and do not change if the allies' type changes later in battle. Prior to version 3.0.1, the effect of Dragon Cheer would remain even if switched in. The move will fail if there is no adjacent ally, or if the ally is already under the effects of Focus Energy or Dragon Cheer. A Dipplin that knows this move will evolve into Hydrapple when leveled up. Dragon Cheer was temporarily banned in official tournaments, due to the bug in Scarlet and Violet 3.0.0 that caused the boost to remain on switching out. The ban was lifted on the release of 3.0.1. Despite its name and description, Dragon Cheer is not a sound-based move.

Title: Alluring Voice (move)

Alluring Voice (Japanese: みわくのボイス Alluring Voice) is a damage-dealing move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is TM227 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Alluring Voice inflicts damage. The move also confuses the target if the target's stats were raised during the same turn before the execution of Alluring Voice. Alluring Voice is the only Fairy-type move introduced in Generation IX to be accessible in regular gameplay.

Title: Temper Flare (move)

Temper Flare (Japanese: やけっぱち Complete Desperation) is a damage-dealing Fire-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is TM207 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Temper Flare is a damage-dealing move. If, on the previous turn, the user's last move missed, failed to affect each of its targets, or was prevented from being used due to an effect such as paralysis, Temper Flare's power is doubled from 75 to 150. If the user was recharging or any target of the user's last move blocked it with a protection move such as Protect on the previous turn, Temper Flare's base power is not doubled. Temper Flare's Fire typing is likely thanks to its Japanese name containing やけ yake, meaning 'burn'. Its English name contains a similar pun on 'flare', while its names in other languages generally opt for a portmanteau instead.

Title: Supercell Slam (move)

Supercell Slam (Japanese: サンダーダイブ Thunder Dive) is a damage-dealing move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is TM210 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Supercell Slam deals damage. If the user misses, the user will take crash damage equal to half of the user's max HP, rounded down. If the target is immune to Supercell Slam by type or Ability (such as Volt Absorb) or is protected, the move counts as a miss. If the target has used Minimize, Supercell Slam deals double its regular damage and bypasses accuracy checks to always hit, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly. This move was first revealed on November 21, 2023 alongside Hard Press prior to the release of The Indigo Disk during Serebii's writeup of their hands-on preview of the DLC, though the move is referred to as Supercharged Cell, possibly erroneously. Supercell Slam is the only move capable of dealing crash damage that is not Fighting-type and the only one that does not have "kick" in its name.

Title: Psychic Noise (move)

Psychic Noise (Japanese: サイコノイズ Psycho Noise) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is TM228 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Psychic Noise deals damage and prevents the target from recovering HP for two turns. During these two turns, if the target uses a move with a healing property, such as Recover or Drain Punch, it will fail. The Heal Block property of this move is considered an additional effect, and is thus blocked by Shield Dust and suppressed by Sheer Force. Aroma Veil also prevents the secondary effect from activating. Using Psychic Noise again while the Heal Block is active will not refresh the duration.

Title: Upper Hand (move)

Upper Hand (Japanese: はやてがえし Quick Hand Reversal) is a damage-dealing Fighting-type move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk. It is TM229 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Upper Hand has a priority of +3. If the target has selected a physical or special move with increased priority from +1 to +3 (including Upper Hand itself) and has not yet had a chance to execute its move, Upper Hand will strike first and cause the target to flinch; otherwise, Upper Hand will fail. It also works on Abilities that give priority to moves, such as Gale Wings. In Pokémon GO, Upper Hand is a Charged Attack that has been available since April 13, 2025. Samurott, Cobalion, Terrakion, Virizion, Keldeo, Talonflame, Falinks, and Flamigo can learn Upper Hand despite lacking hands.

Title: Malignant Chain (move)

Malignant Chain (Japanese: じゃどくのくさり Noxious Chain) is a Poison-type damage-dealing move introduced during Generation IX, in The Indigo Disk (though it was not made available until the release of Mochi Mayhem). It is the signature move of Pecharunt. Malignant Chain inflicts damage and has a 50% chance of badly poisoning the target.

Title: Shadow Rush (move)

Shadow Rush (Japanese: ダークラッシュ Dark Rush) is a move usable only in Pokémon Colosseum and Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. In Pokémon Colosseum, Shadow Rush is the only Shadow move in the game and all Shadow Pokémon know it when captured, while in Pokémon XD, it is one of several Shadow moves. Like all Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. Shadow Rush does damage, with a power of 90, and the Pokémon takes recoil damage equal to ¹⁄₁₆ of the user's maximum HP, rounded down, either directly as the calculated result, or one more or less than it, chosen at random. The recoil damage from this move is not prevented by Rock Head. This move has a 90.625% chance to get a critical hit if the Shadow Pokémon using it is in Hyper Mode. More specifically, it adds a 90% chance that Shadow Rush will land a critical hit, similar to an additional effect. Failing that, it has the usual 6.25% chance to land a critical hit regardless. Despite Shadow moves not being super effective against any type in Colosseum, it will still deal damage to a Pokémon with Wonder Guard. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Colosseum or XD, Shadow Rush cannot be used in Contests. In the Pokémon summary screen, the game still displays a Contest description for this move, with Appeal and Jam scores of 0 each and a blank space where the Contest condition would be. Shadow Rush's base power drops to 55, but it no longer has recoil damage. Hyper Mode no longer exists, and its counterpart, Reverse Mode, does not increase the critical hit ratio. The game no longer displays a Contest description for any moves. All Shadow Pokémon in Pokémon Colosseum know this move from the start. In Pokémon Colosseum, a glitch allows Shadow Rush to hit the opponent when in the semi-invulnerable turn of Fly, Bounce, Dig, and Dive. However, the Shadow Pokémon must be in Hyper Mode, and must first attempt to use Thunder, Surf, Earthquake, or Magnitude accordingly.

Title: Shadow Blast (move)

Shadow Blast (Japanese: ダークブラスト Dark Blast) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. It is the signature move of Shadow Lugia. Shadow Blast is a Shadow version of Aeroblast. It deals damage and has an increased critical hit ratio. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Blast cannot be used in Contests. Despite being based on Aeroblast, Shadow Blast will turn into Psycho Boost upon Shadow Lugia's purification. Although Shadow Blast has the appearance of a special move, it is a physical move because Aeroblast was also a physical move in Generation III. This move is similar in concept to Aeroblast+, the signature move of Apex Shadow Lugia in Pokémon GO. Both are improved variants of Aeroblast exclusive to a specific individual Shadow Pokémon specimen.

Title: Shadow Blitz (move)

Shadow Blitz (Japanese: ダークアタック Dark Attack) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. Shadow Blitz is a Shadow equivalent of the basic damage moves Pokémon often know at Level 1, such as Pound, Scratch, and Tackle. Shadow Blitz inflicts damage and has no secondary effect. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Blitz cannot be used in Contests.

Title: Shadow Bolt (move)

Shadow Bolt (Japanese: ダークサンダー Dark Thunder) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Shadow Pokémon is purified. It is the signature move of Shadow Zapdos. Shadow Bolt is a Shadow version of Thunderbolt. It deals damage and has a 10% chance of inflicting paralysis on the target. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Bolt cannot be used in Contests. Shadow Bolt is the only Shadow move that can cause paralysis.

Title: Shadow Break (move)

Shadow Break (Japanese: ダークブレイク Dark Break) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow Moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. Shadow Break inflicts damage and has no secondary effect. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Break cannot be used in Contests.

Title: Shadow Chill (move)

Shadow Chill (Japanese: ダークフリーズ Dark Freeze) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Shadow Pokémon is purified. It is the signature move of Shadow Articuno. Shadow Chill is a Shadow version of Ice Beam. It deals damage and has a 10% chance of freezing the target. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Chill cannot be used in Contests. Shadow Chill is the only Shadow move that can cause freezing.

Title: Shadow End (move)

Shadow End (Japanese: ダークエンド Dark End) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. Shadow End deals damage, and the user takes recoil damage equal to half of its current HP. The recoil damage from this move is not prevented by Rock Head. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow End cannot be used in Contests. Shadow End has the highest power of all Shadow moves, with 120 base power, and is tied with Shadow Panic for the lowest accuracy, with only 60% accuracy. Shadow End is the only Shadow move in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness that causes recoil damage. Shadow End is used exclusively by Shadow Pokémon owned by Greevil and his two bodyguards/sons Ardos and Eldes.

Title: Shadow Fire (move)

Shadow Fire (Japanese: ダークファイア Dark Fire) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Shadow Pokémon is purified. It is the signature move of Shadow Moltres. Shadow Fire is a Shadow version of Flamethrower. It deals damage and has a 10% chance of burning the target. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Fire cannot be used in Contests. Shadow Fire is the only Shadow move that can cause burning.

Title: Shadow Rave (move)

Shadow Rave (Japanese: ダークレイブ Dark Rave) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Rave cannot be used in Contests.

Title: Shadow Storm (move)

Shadow Storm (Japanese: ダークストーム Dark Storm) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Storm cannot be used in Contests. Shadow Storm has the same power, accuracy, and targeting as Surf in Pokémon XD (the only game this move appears in).

Title: Shadow Wave (move)

Shadow Wave (Japanese: ダークウェーブ Dark Wave) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Wave cannot be used in Contests.

Title: Shadow Down (move)

Shadow Down (Japanese: ダークダウン Dark Down) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. Shadow Down is a Shadow version of Screech. It lowers the Defense of both opponents by two stages each. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Down cannot be used in Contests.

Title: Shadow Half (move)

Shadow Half (Japanese: ダークハーフ Dark Half) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. Shadow Half deals damage equal to 50% of each Pokémon's current HP, rounded down, to all Pokémon in battle, including the user. It cannot cause fainting. The user is forced to recharge during the next turn. Shadow Half cannot be used if the user is under the effect of Taunt. Damage dealt by Shadow Half is counted as an attack for the purpose of Bide, Mirror Coat (but not Counter), and Focus Punch. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Half cannot be used in Contests. Shadow Half is the only damaging move that targets all Pokémon on the field, including the user.

Title: Shadow Hold (move)

Shadow Hold (Japanese: ダークホールド Dark Hold) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. Shadow Hold is a Shadow version of Spider Web, Mean Look, and Block. Shadow Hold targets both opponents and prevents the targets from switching out. The effect only applies as long as the Pokémon that used it remains in battle. Pokémon affected by Shadow Hold can still be forced to switch out with Roar and Whirlwind. The affected Pokémon can still switch out via Baton Pass, but in that case, the Pokémon that switches in will now be affected. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Hold cannot be used in Contests. Shadow Hold is the only trapping move that targets all opponents (but not allies).

Title: Shadow Mist (move)

Shadow Mist (Japanese: ダークミスト Dark Mist) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. Shadow Mist is a Shadow version of Sweet Scent. Shadow Mist is a Shadow version of Sweet Scent. It lowers the evasion of both opponents by two stages each. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Mist cannot be used in Contests.

Title: Shadow Panic (move)

Shadow Panic (Japanese: ダークパニック Dark Panic) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. Shadow Panic is a sound-based move, so it cannot affect Pokémon with Soundproof. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Panic cannot be used in Contests. Shadow Panic is the only Shadow move to be a sound-based move.

Title: Shadow Shed (move)

Shadow Shed (Japanese: ダークリムーブ Dark Remove) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. Shadow Shed removes the effects of Safeguard, Reflect, and Light Screen from the opponent's side of the field. As Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and since Pokémon Contests do not exist in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Shed cannot be used in Contests.

Title: Shadow Sky (move)

Shadow Sky (Japanese: ダークウェザー Dark Weather) is one of several Shadow moves available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Like other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced with another move once the Pokémon is purified. Shadow Sky covers the field in a shadowy aura that lasts for five turns. While in effect, all non-Shadow Pokémon (except those in the semi-invulnerable turn of Dig or Dive) are damaged at the end of each turn by 1/16 of their maximum HP, Shadow moves have their power boosted by 50% (although this part is not mentioned in game), and Weather Ball changes to become typeless and has its power doubled.
